# Thread: Sleep timing
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-timing.28496/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Kendall Toerner — 2024-01-11T03:17:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-timing.28496/post-330081>

I’m curious what everyone’s habits are around sleep timing through seasonal changes.

Do you stay up in winter until 10 pm or so with fire, or do you go to sleep within 1-1.5 hours of sunset?

Sunset here in winter is around 5:30 pm.

If you sleep for more than 7-8 hours a night, how do you manage this?

I personally always wake up after about 8 hours of sleep if I go to bed before 10. I still can fall asleep after waking up, but I toss and turn until very abruptly waking up 5 mins before my backup alarm (30 mins before sunrise).

It’s easy in the summer as I fall asleep around 9:30-10 pm and wake up at sunrise with no gaps in between.

### Joseph LeBlanc — 2024-01-11T14:52:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-timing.28496/post-330090>

I go to sleep 2-3 hrs after sunset. For my location, that is around 7:30-8:30. I typically lay down between 8-8:30

Bc of my work schedule, I have to wake at 5am. If I had a choice, I would sleep in til about 6-6:30, which is roughly 30 min before sunrise.

Sometimes I wake in the middle of the night. When this happens, I usually just get up, walk around a bit, and get a drink of water. Then, I lay back down and usually fall back asleep no problem.

If I wake within ~1 hr. of the time I planned to wake, I will usually just get up for good and do some light reading under dim red or candle light.

I usually stay asleep the full night though. For the times I don't, I think it is usually caused by poor meal timing. I have a bad habit of eating within 1-2 hours before bed (7-8 pm) sometimes. When I do that, I typically wake during the night.

### kris90 — 2024-01-11T17:05:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-timing.28496/post-330098>

Right now sun sets at 4:40 pm, and rises at 7:40 am.

First sleep 7:00 pm to 12:00 am (deep sleep/cellular repair)
Second sleep 1:00 am to 5:00 am (dream state)

### Kendall Toerner — 2024-01-11T18:13:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-timing.28496/post-330100>

What I don't quite understand is how it's appropriate to wake up before any sunlight is present in the sky, since the sun is responsible for waking us up and initiating so many functions in our body

### kris90 — 2024-01-11T18:56:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-timing.28496/post-330103>

> [Kendall Toerner said:](/goto/post?id=330100)
>
> What I don't quite understand is how it's appropriate to wake up before any sunlight is present in the sky, since the sun is responsible for waking us up and initiating so many functions in our body

Sun is not responsible for waking us up. Our natural diurnal cortisol rhythm is. And its not just regulated by light, but also by temperature. Since it's winter right now, if we are properly abiding by our circadian biology, we are not coupled to the light cycle and are supposed to be using temperature as the dominant circadian stimulator. Hence why biphasic sleeping is the norm in winter months because cold/dark environments upregulate autophagy.

### Kendall Toerner — 2024-01-11T19:01:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-timing.28496/post-330104>

Since it's winter right now, if we are properly abiding by our circadian biology, we are not coupled to the light cycle and are supposed to be using temperature as the dominant circadian stimulator. Hence why biphasic sleeping is the norm in winter months because cold/dark environments upregulate autophagy.

AH I see, I remember some of this from the CT blogs. Seems I need to re-read them again. Very fascinating. Thank you for the quick explanation.

# Thread: The pathways how cold invokes adaptations
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-pathways-how-cold-invokes-adaptations.28498/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### sfast — 2024-01-12T11:41:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-pathways-how-cold-invokes-adaptations.28498/post-330123>

Dear Freezers,

I am diving deeper into how cold creates signals for adaptation. I use various sources of cold:

1\. Ice bath
2\. Cold Air (during Jogging)
3\. Cooling Vest (still looking for a nice one)

There are different sites of cold being translated into a signal: Cold receptors (deep and surface), core body temperature etc.

I'd like to create a complete(ish) list of how cold creates the effect. The ice bath for example is a general assault on all pathways I think. But cryotherapy for example mainly cools down your surface (if I got it right). Also: There is a difference between mild cold stress like Ray Cronise promotes and intense cold training like ice baths.

This is my list (in paranthesis is my tool):

1\. Surface cold receptors (ice bath, cold air with little clothing)
2\. Deep cold receptors (ice bath)
3\. The disruption of temperature homöostasis, short term/steep (are there thresholds?) (ice bath)
4\. The disruption of temperature homöostasis, long term/mild (cold air, cold vest)
5\. Direct cold stress on BAT (cold vest)
6\. Disruption of energy homöostasis, short term (ice bath, combination of running and cold air)
7\. Disruption of energy homöostasis, long term (cool vest)

I am still in an explorative stage. So, feel free to throw in everything that might be missing.

Some of the potential tools I seek:

1\. Vastly increase the amount of cold stress and avoid interference with strength training (strength in the morning, cooling vest till noon possible if not intense?)
2\. Being able to quantify the total stimulus and having multiple sites of progression.
3\. Optimising the total amount of cold stress and coordinating the various sources of cold. (is it better to have a short ice bath and prolong the cold via vest?)

I would be eternally thankful for any paper, studies, books that you throw at me.

### Jack Kruse — 2024-01-12T19:10:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-pathways-how-cold-invokes-adaptations.28498/post-330133>

look at the cites in the CT series of blogs. There are plenty

### sfast — 2024-01-14T08:44:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-pathways-how-cold-invokes-adaptations.28498/post-330203>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=330133)
>
> look at the cites in the CT series of blogs. There are plenty

I am pretty much a fanboy of the early days (early 2010s). So, I read the CT series two times already.  (Many thanks for your work, btw.)

The extraction of your references and my try of summarising and presenting your work to the German audience is part of my project of ordering all the material of CT out there.

My goal is to integrate the various aspects of CT by using the framework of Conscilience of Knowledge by E.O. Wilson. (If you didn't read it, I'd be surprise if this wouldn't be a fun read considering your style of thinking)

The challenge I have is that your material is informed by the distinction of health and disease. My CT practice is mostly informed by your work with a reading primary literature as additional material. I know first hand that your framework is effective way beyond moving from sick to healthy. The thresholds of benefits are for example higher from my experience than currently presented (Søberg and Huberman did a podcast which I feel goes in a wrong direction how much you could benefit from a more intense CT-approach). But to connect your work to a more general audience (mainstream science, crossfit, etc. for example) there is a lot of work to be done to create understandable bridges. So, this is what I am working on: A slow cognitive path of seduction instead of just slamming a quantum slap into the face of people.

I pretty much search for the boring stuff like "Comparison ice bath compared to cold air, equalized for total heat loss"

This is the gap I see in the collective understanding.

### Jack Kruse — 2024-01-14T18:18:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-pathways-how-cold-invokes-adaptations.28498/post-330213>

Cold affects ROS and RNS and NAD+ and this stimulates melanin to move and be degraded in the absence of UV light. This is how catecholamines are raised by cold. That is all the decentralized science that you jave to know that is in between the words of the CT series.

The MELANIN renovation blog will hit that up

### Jack Kruse — 2024-01-15T21:34:00+0200

### sfast — 2024-01-16T17:36:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-pathways-how-cold-invokes-adaptations.28498/post-330297>

Many thanks [@Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/) (I still hope that you start to work with elite athletes, so your work can trickle down to the masses)

If you are ever interested in the Zettelkasten Method. My service will be free of charge for you.

Right now, I have 170 open tasks to complete the first row of research on cold (one task might be "read book XY"). When I moved to a point of having enough substance to share it, I will.

# Thread: Need Help. Blood pressure increases when exposed to sunlight
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-help-blood-pressure-increases-when-exposed-to-sunlight.28499/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Shivanand Tendulker — 2024-01-12T18:01:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-help-blood-pressure-increases-when-exposed-to-sunlight.28499/post-330129>

Hello,

I live at 12.9716° N latitude. I like to exercise outdoors. What I observed recently (6 months back) that my blood pressure goes up when I do exercise (Yoga) in Sun. I came to know about this when I was declined to donate blood due to high BP. I do exercise in the morning (9:00AM -10:30AM). I do not get any fatigue or tiredness through sun exposure. The blood pressure shoots up to 130+ /90+ mm/Hg when measured at night for the days I exercise. If I skip exercise in sunlight it takes about 2-3 days for blood pressure to normalize to 120/80 mm/Hg. I did my complete blood tests related to Lipid, Homogram, Biochemistry, Lipid profile, Liver function and thyroid. Everything seems within range. Apart from elevated blood pressure there is no other symptom.

I would like to seek inputs from this community on what is that I could be missing here.

Thank you,
Shiv

### Jack Kruse — 2024-01-12T19:09:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-help-blood-pressure-increases-when-exposed-to-sunlight.28499/post-330132>

exercise and yoga do increase BP, the sun does not.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-help-blood-pressure-increases-when-exposed-to-sunlight.28499/post-330134>

Listen to what I say at the 55:00 minute here.

# Thread: Covid 19 vaxed sperm
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/covid-19-vaxed-sperm.28500/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### DLO — 2024-01-12T22:05:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/covid-19-vaxed-sperm.28500/post-330142>

**Sperm cells in males who have received a Covid-19 injection are being replaced by spike proteins, a German medical examiner has disturbingly claimed.**
During a recent lecture, the [late pathologist Prof. Dr. Arne Burkhardt](https://medicotopics.com/dr-arne-burkhardt-death-pathologist-professor-dies-aged-79/) explained how after examining tissue samples from both young and old men who died following vaccination, he discovered spike proteins had overtaken sperm producing organs and the sperm itself.

“Here you see the case where we show the testes,” Burkhardt said, pointing to a slide, “and you can see that in this 28-year-old man who had a healthy son and who died 140 days after injection, **the spike protein is in the strongly expressed in the spermatogenic organ in the testes, and you can see there are almost no spermatozides in here,** but it’s strongly expression of spike protein in the spermatoconic tissue.”

Dr. Burkhardt next described the same issue with the older patient, saying, “So also in the older person, this is an old man, and you can see here also a strong expression in the spermatogonia, **there’s not one single spermatozoon in this**, and a strong expression of the spike protein.”
The forensic pathologist went on to caution women against producing offspring with vaccinated males, saying, “If I were a woman in fertile age, I would not plan a motherhood from a person, from a man who has been vaccinated.”
**_RELATED — [Demand for ‘Unvaxxed’ Sperm Soars: Women Looking For Donors Who Refused Experimental Covid Jab](https://www.infowars.com/posts/demand-for-unvaxxed-sperm-soars-women-looking-for-donors-who-refused-experimental-covid-jab/)_**

The research coincides with other data showing female ovaries are also destroyed by the spike protein present in the mRNA shots, suggesting the jabs prompt a complete overhaul of the human reproduction system.

Dr. Burkhardt’s observations demonstrate how Covid vaccines are having a devastating impact on humanity and the [reproduction of our species](https://www.infowars.com/posts/the-link-between-the-massive-drop-in-birth-rates-and-mrna-vaccines-is-undeniable/). At the very least, his discoveries warrant further investigation on the subject.

### Jack Kruse — 2024-01-12T23:43:11+0200

I'd sell mine but it wont come cheap

# Thread: Letting the light in...
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/letting-the-light-in.28501/>
**Subforum:** Feedback/Suggestions
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Plural — 2024-01-12T22:05:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/letting-the-light-in.28501/post-330143>

I've been making sure a window or two is cracked at home, even during winter to receive scattered sunlight, however, the office I am currently in does not have windows that open (what a fail).

On typical work day, I see sunrise at home then commute 40min to office with windows open entire drive. Will get out for a 15min walk at lunch. (Also wearing less layers on walk for past few months than I would have previously when temperatures are lower.)

It sounds like the best ways to compensate for the lack of sunlight during most of the day would be:

1) Blue blocking lenses when working at computer (Video Editor)
2) Some form of daily CT
3) Prioritizing foods that are in season (Ohio)
4) Natural movement practice outdoors during daylight when not at office

There are days when I've been able to WFH and have the computer set up on porch. This is ideal for me and I enjoy that when I can.

Open to other thoughts/suggestions and/or things to avoid outside of putting in my 2 weeks and starting a landscaping business.

### Anthony Smith — 2024-01-13T03:35:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/letting-the-light-in.28501/post-330163>

Have you checked your nnEMF levels both at home (bedroom) and office? Not only RF (important) but also electric and magnetic fields. Lots of info here, follow the links: [www.LiveEMFSafe.com/kruse](http://www.LiveEMFSafe.com/kruse)

### Plural — 2024-01-17T18:33:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/letting-the-light-in.28501/post-330327>

I will double check these. I know it is important as well.

Question was more directed at mediating for lack of sun, or ways to get more sun (obviously working outdoors is best solution).

But yes, #5 could be low-EMF workzone. Thanks!

> 2) Some form of daily CT
> 3) Prioritizing foods that are in season (Ohio)
> 4) Natural movement practice outdoors during daylight when not at office
>
> There are days when I've been able to WFH and have the computer set up on porch. This is ideal for me and I enjoy that when I can.
>
> Open to other thoughts/suggestions and/or things to avoid outside of putting in my 2 weeks and starting a landscaping business.

I live in Indiana and work for a tech company. I feel our day to days are very similar. My work windows do not open either. So unfortunate. I am curious as to what other mediations can be taken for our locations and work. Best to you!

### Anthony Smith — 2024-01-17T21:10:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/letting-the-light-in.28501/post-330338>

> [jules3791 said:](/goto/post?id=330328)
>
> I live in Indiana and work for a tech company. I feel our day to days are very similar. My work windows do not open either. So unfortunate. I am curious as to what other mediations can be taken for our locations and work. Best to you!

I have both a NIR (near infrared) light and visible red light behind my screen to help counter (neutralize) the blue light from the screen, as well as adjusting the screen colors to less blue more red.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/letting-the-light-in.28501/post-330348>

Thank you so much! Do you have links to purchase these items?

### Marko Pollo — 2024-01-18T19:29:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/letting-the-light-in.28501/post-330352>

Don't be afraid of a landscape company or any outdoor physical labor. The narrative is that smart people use their heads to make more money with less work. That is a trap for fools.

### Anthony Smith — 2024-01-18T21:42:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/letting-the-light-in.28501/post-330360>

> [jules3791 said:](/goto/post?id=330348)
>
> Thank you so much! Do you have links to purchase these items?

There is a lot of info on the forum about different types of NIR lights. I cannot comment on them because I do not have any of them. Some of them are only 850nm others offer additional frequencies, they also cost $$$. What I have is a 850nm NIR security light from Amazon. Works for me. There are many other companies to choose from but I have 2 of these, CMVision also have smaller ones. They do not include the power supply so order the CMVision power supply they recommend for each light. $50 vs $500 ;-)

[www.amazon.com/CMVision-IRP24-850nm-WideAngle-Power-Illuminator/dp/B00Q15Z5U8/ref=sr\_1\_6?crid=2AN78LVKM3M0K&dib=eyJ2IjoiMSJ9.fP3VNFWHVOWVyrVPIoa6fiUus3LXYz1OodIvTA4B8tI063nrfelOoDuPwFbq3J73OJbO\_XBr5GLTEKlcgxMGMe1NEv1ZtfzbFkdcc7uHG5Tpt\_baAPfQfK2](http://www.amazon.com/CMVision-IRP24-850nm-WideAngle-Power-Illuminator/dp/B00Q15Z5U8/ref=sr_1_6?crid=2AN78LVKM3M0K&dib=eyJ2IjoiMSJ9.fP3VNFWHVOWVyrVPIoa6fiUus3LXYz1OodIvTA4B8tI063nrfelOoDuPwFbq3J73OJbO_XBr5GLTEKlcgxMGMe1NEv1ZtfzbFkdcc7uHG5Tpt_baAPfQfK2)

### annav — 2024-12-26T19:43:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/letting-the-light-in.28501/post-344363>

> [rGebura said:](/goto/post?id=330143)
>
> I've been making sure a window or two is cracked at home, even during winter to receive scattered sunlight, however, the office I am currently in does not have windows that open (what a fail).
>
> On typical work day, I see sunrise at home then commute 40min to office with windows open entire drive. Will get out for a 15min walk at lunch. (Also wearing less layers on walk for past few months than I would have previously when temperatures are lower.)
>
> It sounds like the best ways to compensate for the lack of sunlight during most of the day would be:
>
> 1) Blue blocking lenses when working at computer (Video Editor)
> 2) Some form of daily CT
> 3) Prioritizing foods that are in season (Ohio)
> 4) Natural movement practice outdoors during daylight when not at office
>
> There are days when I've been able to WFH and have the computer set up on porch. This is ideal for me and I enjoy that when I can.
>
> Open to other thoughts/suggestions and/or things to avoid outside of putting in my 2 weeks and starting a landscaping business.

I am in Ostrander, Ohio so I am with you. Do you have a dog? I have a dog who is part Husky and he needs 4-5 walks a day. It definitely gets me outside. I also wear grounding shoes on the walk, and I walk in wet grass whenever possible. I always have a window cracked and I always eat by the open window with red light panel on my abdomen. This way I am getting red light and some scattered UV light. Other than that, I try to plan a trip south in January or February.

# Thread: I'm going to heal my feet!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-going-to-heal-my-feet.28502/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 179
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### NicklasK — 2024-01-13T10:02:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-going-to-heal-my-feet.28502/post-330166>

I'm relatively new to Jack Kruse's work, and I only wish I had found it sooner.

I'm a 41 year old man living in Denmark. For 10 years, I have been crawling on my knees and using a wheelchair because my feet have a neurological problem.

it started after years of stressful work doing undergraduate, graduate and PhD work in nanoscience, including a serious concussion in a car crash, which I was however able to mostly heal. I was also doing standard dancing at a competition level, as well as weight lifting - not to become bodybuilder-muscular, but to get athletically muscular.

I've been through the medical system, gotten a MRI scan and x-rays, and the closest thing I got to a diagnosis was CRPS (complex regional pain syndrome). Basically my feet are extremely easily sprained/strained, and I have been unable to train them to a level where I can start walking again, without the sprain/strain recurring.

I'm doing the following things (plus a bunch more), most of which are from Jack's recommendations:

\- eating a lot of wild-caught fish, mostly canned, especially sardines, cod roe, cod liver, and also oysters a couple of times a month
\- eating mostly home-grown vegetables such as onions, cabbage, swiss chard, green beans
\- eating seaweed, mostly nori, but also some arame, and trying out various of the types Jack recommends
\- I get 5 minutes of bright light soon after waking at 4-6 am, however this is with a Beurer light therapy device which is the best I can do at the moment since the sun doesn't rise until 9.30 am at this time of year
\- I get outside and watch the sunrise for 5-10 minutes, and if the water isn't frozen, I dip my head in a bowl of water, and then do as many knee-squats as I can
\- I watch the sunset, usually with my shirt off, and do some more knee squats
\- I don't look at screens after 6 pm, I start wearing blue blockers ad 7 pm, and I go to bed at 9 pm
\- I grow and drink wheatgrass juice, this has helped significantly with my energy and skin issues (I previously had acne)
\- I use a "block blue light" red light device for 20 minutes a day
\- I massage my entire body with foam rollers and have just started doing the "block therapy" program which is based on the same principle
\- I started eating 1-4 tsp of sea salt a day

Things are improving somewhat with my feet, but it still feels like I have to be very careful with how I use them.

Am I missing any low-hanging fruit? (seasonally and climate appropriate fruit, of course)

Or even higher-hanging fruit would be great as well, if it has a good chance at healing me.

I'm also considering a Sperti Fiji light, as I've read that some people here recommend them.

I'm still getting too much artificial light, however I have set my computer screen at 3000K, and using incandescent or 2700K LED light bulbs around the house. I'm working on improving this area - that is, getting more natural sunlight and less artificial.

### coho3131 — 2024-01-13T17:19:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-going-to-heal-my-feet.28502/post-330187>

Hello. If it was me, I would try for more sun, less artificial light, more outdoor time with less clothing, more CT, and not eating any vegetables that are not still growing outside.

### JanSz — 2024-01-13T22:50:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-going-to-heal-my-feet.28502/post-330192>

> [NicklasK said:](/goto/post?id=330166)
>
> I'm a 41 year old man living in Denmark. For 10 years, I have been crawling on my knees and using a wheelchair because my feet have a neurological problem.
>
> it started after years of stressful work doing undergraduate, graduate and PhD work in nanoscience, including a serious concussion in a car crash, which I was however able to mostly heal. I was also doing standard dancing at a competition level,
>
> I'm also considering a Sperti Fiji light, as I've read that some people here recommend them.

I am jealous of all dancers. My wife used to be balerina (and then she laid her eyes on me). Swan Lake
Until recently, her friend (Black Swan), after retired from balet, she was teaching standard dancers how to look presentable.

I would check:

Uric acid (in the blood) (and make sure that it is less than 4, naturally) (no high fructose additives in religious ways) (fruits and their fructose ok)
homocysteine (and make sure that it is 6-7
potassium RBC (and make sure that it is nicely above low laboratory level)

Red Sperti (start easy) go to once daily on the full timer, them possibly longer

250W Heat Lamp Red Infrared, shining on you naked feet and lower legs all the time you are sitting in front of your laptop. Close but do not get burned.

### NikkiYork — 2024-01-14T07:22:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-going-to-heal-my-feet.28502/post-330202>

Not sure how low those fruit are hanging for you, but maybe get a vacation home? Or move?
If that’s been going on for 10 years already, man, I’m sorry to hear, but maybe change something drastically?
I moved from Germany to the US when I was 33. Alone, no job, not a person I knew. But I could walk, so maybe it’s different for you.
And I’m still too far north
So maybe you could get yourself to the lower latitudes at least temporarily?

### NicklasK — 2024-01-14T13:14:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-going-to-heal-my-feet.28502/post-330206>

\---
coho3131:
\---

Yes, I believe you are right. I am working on increasing these elements.

\---
Anthony Smith:
\---

Thank you - I have recently bought a IR/red light unit from Block Blue Light, and have started using it daily.

\---
JanSz:
\---

I haven't done ballet myself, but I have a lot of respect for good ballet dancers.

Thank you, I will get those markers checked the next time I get a blood test.

I am definitely going to get the Sperti, and looking forward to seeing what effects it has. I've also considered trying a 250w red heat lamp on my feet so I will do that soon too.

\---
NikkiYork:
\---

Yes, I have thought about moving to a warm country for at least a good while, and it's definitely not off the table.

However, I would like to try all the obvious things that might fix the situation while staying here for now - and if that is not enough, then I will probably move at some point.

I believe that I am very optimized for my local climate, with my genetics going back a long time in this area, and I dislike being in warm countries as I have zero energy when it gets hot. I thrive in cool temperatures, however I'm not yet really good at dealing with intense cold, so I am working on gradually increasing my CT training.

\---
Brent Patrick:
\---

Thank you very much for that link! I got very excited reading it, as there were several questions answered from people with problems highly similar to mine!

One thing mentioned for CRPS was depleted deuterium water, and I have just bought some 25 ppm DDW that I haven't started drinking yet. It's extremely expensive! I bought it from biopure.eu, and it ended up costing 74 usd/gal, or 133 kr/L.

Does anyone know how to get it significantly cheaper, preferably in the EU?

\---

I have an Oura ring which tells me that my HRV is in the 25-42 range, which is very low. I believe Jack says that a low HRV is often caused by sunlight deficiency, which would definitely make sense in my situation.

I have it on flight mode all the time except for a few minutes in the morning while I upload the data to my cell phone, with the ring and the phone being in a Faraday bag. I believe this should cause minimal nnEMF problems. It still collects data from the body while it is on flight mode, it just doesn't try to communicate with the phone.

I usually have a sleep score in the 78-92 range, and recovery scores on the 80-90 range on the Oura.

Also, I didn't mention yet that I have Dupuytren's contracture in my left hand. I had a severe sprain in it 8 years ago, and since then I've been unable to straighten the fingers more than about 110 degrees from the hand. There was a question in the neuropathy QnA part with someone having almost exactly the same symptoms, and Jack recommended DDW, sunlight and CT, so now I'm very optimistic that this can also be healed!

I had a severe sprain in it 8 years ago, and since then I've been unable to straighten the fingers more than about 110 degrees from the hand. There was a question in the neuropathy QnA part with someone having almost exactly the same symptoms, and Jack recommended DDW, sunlight and CT, so now I'm very optimistic that this can also be healed!

Natural water from a well, spring, or stream at mountain altitude from your region might be a good choice for water. If you can't find something from a private source, Gerolsteiner in glass bottles comes to mind as being reasonably local for you.

If it was me I would not be using the Oura ring even in flight mode. When you get your nnEMF meters, you could test it to be sure.

How close to an ocean beach are you?

### JanSz — 2024-01-15T00:49:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-going-to-heal-my-feet.28502/post-330230>

**what are the reasons for Neuropathy**

Neuropathy, a condition that affects the nerves, can be caused by a variety of factors:

1.  [**Diabetes**: Diabetic peripheral neuropathy is the most common type of neuropathy, affecting nearly 60% of people with diabetes](https://www.everydayhealth.com/neuropathy/guide/causes/)[1](https://www.everydayhealth.com/neuropathy/guide/causes/).
2.  [**Vitamin B deficiency**: A lack of certain vitamins, particularly B12, can lead to neuropathy](https://www.everydayhealth.com/neuropathy/guide/causes/)[2](https://www.msn.com/en-us/health/condition/Neuropathy/hp-Neuropathy?source=conditioncdx).
3.  [**Autoimmune diseases**: Conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis can cause neuropathy](https://www.everydayhealth.com/neuropathy/guide/causes/)[2](https://www.msn.com/en-us/health/condition/Neuropathy/hp-Neuropathy?source=conditioncdx).
4.  [**Injury to nerves**: Physical damage to the nerves, such as from an injury or during surgery, can result in neuropathy](https://www.everydayhealth.com/neuropathy/guide/causes/)[3](https://www.nhs.uk/conditions/peripheral-neuropathy/causes/).
5.  [**Infectious diseases**: Certain infections, such as HIV/AIDS, leprosy, syphilis, and post-herpetic neuralgia (a complication resulting from shingles), can lead to neuropathy](https://www.msn.com/en-us/health/condition/Neuropathy/hp-Neuropathy?source=conditioncdx)[2](https://www.msn.com/en-us/health/condition/Neuropathy/hp-Neuropathy?source=conditioncdx).
6.  [**Genetic disorders**: Certain inherited conditions, such as Friedreich’s ataxia, can cause neuropathy](https://www.everydayhealth.com/neuropathy/guide/causes/)[2](https://www.msn.com/en-us/health/condition/Neuropathy/hp-Neuropathy?source=conditioncdx).
7.  [**Amyloidosis**: This condition, in which abnormal protein fibers are deposited in tissues and organs, can lead to neuropathy](https://www.msn.com/en-us/health/condition/Neuropathy/hp-Neuropathy?source=conditioncdx)[2](https://www.msn.com/en-us/health/condition/Neuropathy/hp-Neuropathy?source=conditioncdx).
8.  [**Tumors**: Tumors located close to nerves can cause neuropathy](https://www.msn.com/en-us/health/condition/Neuropathy/hp-Neuropathy?source=conditioncdx)[2](https://www.msn.com/en-us/health/condition/Neuropathy/hp-Neuropathy?source=conditioncdx).
9.  [**Alcohol abuse**: Excessive alcohol consumption can lead to neuropathy](https://www.msn.com/en-us/health/condition/Neuropathy/hp-Neuropathy?source=conditioncdx)[2](https://www.msn.com/en-us/health/condition/Neuropathy/hp-Neuropathy?source=conditioncdx).
10.  [**Certain medications or drugs**: Some drugs, particularly those used to treat cancer and HIV/AIDS, can cause neuropathy](https://www.msn.com/en-us/health/condition/Neuropathy/hp-Neuropathy?source=conditioncdx)[2](https://www.msn.com/en-us/health/condition/Neuropathy/hp-Neuropathy?source=conditioncdx)[4](https://www.foundationforpn.org/causes/).

### NicklasK — 2024-01-15T14:39:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-going-to-heal-my-feet.28502/post-330249>

\---
JanSz:
\---

Seems like you've gotten to some very good levels of deuterium by drinking 500 mL a day - I will probably also do this.

I also took Lugol's for a while (2-4 drops a day, 7% Lugol's), then stopped, but have just started again.

I have also eaten very low amounts of LA for years. I got my blood tested for pufas a while ago, and I had a n-6/n-3 of 2.0.

\---
coho3131 & Anthony Smith:
\---

There are no mountains in Denmark, and I don't really trust most bottled water. It does not seem that Gerolsteiner is available in Denmark. I've been drinking distilled water for a while, and have recently bought an RO system.

So the current plan is to drink distilled & RO water and add 500 mL of 25 ppm DDW daily. I am also adding orthosilicic acid to the water as per the instructions in Dennis Crouse's book, "Silica Water the Secret of Healthy Longevity in the Aluminum Age", mainly to counter the effects of ubiquitous aluminum, as you can probably tell from the title of the book.

I already have some nnEMF meters:

Cornet ED78S - measures radiofrequency, and AC magnetic fields
Gigahertz 3030B - measures AC electric and magnetic fields
A simple AM radio - measures primarily dirty electricity/transients produced by e.g. switch-mode power supplies and inverters

I tested the ring with all 3. When it is not on flight mode, it produces significant amounts of rf, but on flight mode, I don't detect anything with any of the meters. I wouldn't expect any AC fields anyway, though, as Anthony points out.

The ring does produce visible light part of the time, but the light looks unchanged when I'm looking at it with my blue blockers, so for now I assume that it does nothing significantly negative. I might however stop wearing it daily and just use it for a couple days a month to see if my baseline has changed.

I'm about 50 km from a beach. However, we do have a vacation home at the beach that I will go to in summer and get a lot of sun and saltwater.

By the way, I can stay in the water a lot longer in a wetsuit - would it be best for my health to only be in the water a short while without the wetsuit, or a lot longer with it on? I would of course take it off when taking breaks on the beach.

>

> **n-6/n-3 of 2.0.**

That is a optimistic starting point.
But really it is about:
1\. LA/ALA being really close to 1 or at least 2
2\. sum of ((Omega-6)+(Omega-3)) being less than some metabolizing capacity of your body
Note what is involved in metabolizing n-6 and n-3. All those minerals, vitamins cofactors. (See the picture below)
Body, when was about 140# and 5' tall (in 1850 and before) was able to metabolize some amounts of n6 & n-3.
I am sure it was able to metabolize much more, had an excellent reserve, assume it was able to metabolize twice as much or 10x that much.
But when you start talking about 100 or even 1000, that screws the system.
Also,
if you strat concentrating say on LA (Linoleic Acid), you find out that its half life is about two years.
So, most people high on LA need religious abstinence of LA for about 6 years to see any results. Not easy to happen.

But your current n-6/n-3 of 2.0 at least looks promising.
\------
One more, be careful with attempting supplementation of individual n-6 fatty acids.
The supplements advertise one of the n-6 (that you may need or are low on it.
But they rarely or never tell you that as a "bonus" you, are getting unacceptable amounts of LA.
Example--- Evening Primrose oil
as a supplement of (Gamma-Linoleic Acid (GLA)

I was also doing standard dancing at a competition level, as well as weight lifting - not to become bodybuilder-muscular, but to get athletically muscular.
>
> I've been through the medical system, gotten a MRI scan and x-rays, and the closest thing I got to a diagnosis was CRPS (complex regional pain syndrome). Basically my feet are extremely easily sprained/strained, and I have been unable to train them to a level where I can start walking again, without the sprain/strain recurring.
>
> I'm doing the following things (plus a bunch more), most of which are from Jack's recommendations:
>
> \- eating a lot of wild-caught fish, mostly canned, especially sardines, cod roe, cod liver, and also oysters a couple of times a month
> \- eating mostly home-grown vegetables such as onions, cabbage, swiss chard, green beans
> \- eating seaweed, mostly nori, but also some arame, and trying out various of the types Jack recommends
> \- I get 5 minutes of bright light soon after waking at 4-6 am, however this is with a Beurer light therapy device which is the best I can do at the moment since the sun doesn't rise until 9.30 am at this time of year
> \- I get outside and watch the sunrise for 5-10 minutes, and if the water isn't frozen, I dip my head in a bowl of water, and then do as many knee-squats as I can
> \- I watch the sunset, usually with my shirt off, and do some more knee squats
> \- I don't look at screens after 6 pm, I start wearing blue blockers ad 7 pm, and I go to bed at 9 pm
> \- I grow and drink wheatgrass juice, this has helped significantly with my energy and skin issues (I previously had acne)
> \- I use a "block blue light" red light device for 20 minutes a day
> \- I massage my entire body with foam rollers and have just started doing the "block therapy" program which is based on the same principle
> \- I started eating 1-4 tsp of sea salt a day
>
> Things are improving somewhat with my feet, but it still feels like I have to be very careful with how I use them.
>
> Am I missing any low-hanging fruit? (seasonally and climate appropriate fruit, of course)
>
> Or even higher-hanging fruit would be great as well, if it has a good chance at healing me.
>
> I'm also considering a Sperti Fiji light, as I've read that some people here recommend them.
>
> I'm still getting too much artificial light, however I have set my computer screen at 3000K, and using incandescent or 2700K LED light bulbs around the house. I'm working on improving this area - that is, getting more natural sunlight and less artificial.

**relationship between NEUROPATHY and Subluxation**

The relationship between neuropathy and subluxation can be complex and is often dependent on the specifics of the individual case.
Here are some key points to understand:

Subluxation refers to the misalignment or abnormal positioning of the vertebrae in your spine1.
This can lead to loss of function due to different pressure points on your spinal column1.
Neuropathy is a term that refers to general diseases or malfunctions of the nerves2.
If the subluxation is severe enough to cause pressure on the spinal cord or nerves, it could potentially lead to symptoms such as pain, tingling, numbness, and muscle weakness2.
**This could be considered a form of neuropathy, as it involves nerve dysfunction.

//////////////////////**

### NicklasK — 2024-01-17T17:16:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-going-to-heal-my-feet.28502/post-330324>

\---
JanSz:
\---

I've eaten very little LA for more than 10 years, compared to the general western population. I do not eat seed oils, rarely eat pork fat. My main sources of LA are moderate amounts of almonds and olive oil, and those are not foods that I am concerned about.

So Jack specifically recommended evening primrose oil in that article on peripheral neuropathy. It does have very high levels of LA, namely 70-74%.
while Borage has 40%.

Here's an interesting article about the effects of evening primrose oil, borage etc on peripheral neuropathy in diabetic rats:

[Checking your browser - reCAPTCHA](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/8931113/)

"1% blackcurrant, borage, fungal and evening primrose dietary supplementation caused 11%, 32%, 41% and 53% NCV ameliorations, respectively."

So it seems that it might be a good idea to use evening primrose oil despite its high LA content, and be very mindful to limit LA from other sources, and get sufficient omega-3 oil to balance it.

So what amount to take? One random source mentioned 1.4 g/day of evening primrose oil, and the rat study used 1-2% of their diet in the form of the oil.

I'm guessing that 1/2-1 tsp a day might be a good dose, with the full teaspoon also giving about 3.3 g of LA.

I'm indeed also considering spending some time in a warm country such as El Salvador, but I want to first try everything possible that I can do here.

About the chiropractor info, I have been to one to see if it would have any effect on my feet, and unfortunately it didn't. I may just not have found a chiropractor that was skilled enough though.

I suggest that before you start evening primrose oil,
first you do fatty acid analysis.
But it has to be the kind that will give you content of individual fatty acids.
I am doing this test:
**Fatty Acid Profile, Comprehensive (C8-C26), Serum**

Eventually you have to rely on the values but be rather leery about suggested laboratory acceptable ranges. Not having much choice, for myself I use middle of those ranges.
Using those middle ranges my laboratory likes LA/ALA ratio of bout 34.
By now we know that they have no clue.
But what is even worst, for most people (who posted results of their tests here), that 34 is very hard to get.

Keep in mind that excessive seed oils feeds human population for about 200 years already. So number of generations.
But the oldest laboratories are something like 50 years old.
They have never seen a person not overloaded with seed oils.

Very few people even think about individual fatty acids.
Even Jack uses PUFA to talk about it.

Very likely scientists that work on developing the animal feed know more about it.
Long time ago they figured that feeding seed oils to the animal make them grow (extremely) fast.
But nobody cares about a health of a chicken that is about to be slaughtered.
Except that they know when the chic will be dying and recommend killing them one or two days before that.

\============
So, when you get that kind of test you get some baseline.
Pick up some dose that sounds resonably,
observe if you feel some differences,
some months latter repeat fatty acid test.
\=============
Every week I see my chiropractor. Sometimes 2x a week.
Will ask him about you.
Can you describe your problem in one or two short lines?
(He has no clue about fatty acids.)
Just press here or there, something (hopefully good) happens.
He got me out from surgeon's scalpel, decades ago, so I do not complain.

### JanSz — 2024-01-20T16:01:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-going-to-heal-my-feet.28502/post-330407>

> [NicklasK said:](/goto/post?id=330403)
>
> My main problem in one line:
>
> Chronic sprains/strains and stiffness in my feet that relapses when I try to gradually rehabilitate them.

Below is not from my chiropractor.
I am just trying to study what is contained on interned in that regard.
\========================

Chronic sprains/strains and stiffness in my feet that relapses when I try to gradually rehabilitate them.

Searching for: how to rehabilitate chronic sprains and strains in feet

Here are some general suggestions for rehabilitating chronic sprains and strains in the feet, based on various sources:

1.  **Rest**: Avoid activities that worsen your pain. [This might mean you must avoid putting weight on your foot and use crutches to walk](https://www.verywellhealth.com/sprained-foot-7506255)[1](https://www.verywellhealth.com/sprained-foot-7506255)[2](https://www.wikihow.com/Heal-a-Foot-Sprain).
2.  **Ice**: Apply ice to your foot for about 20 minutes several times daily. [Place a towel or cloth between your skin and the ice pack to help prevent ice burn](https://www.verywellhealth.com/sprained-foot-7506255)[1](https://www.verywellhealth.com/sprained-foot-7506255).
3.  **Compression**: Wrap your foot with an elastic bandage to support the injured ligaments and reduce swelling. [Be sure you can fit two fingertips between the bandage and your skin to ensure the wrap is not too tight](https://www.verywellhealth.com/sprained-foot-7506255)[1](https://www.verywellhealth.com/sprained-foot-7506255).
4.  [**Elevation**: Prop your foot above the level of your heart, if possible, to help reduce swelling](https://www.verywellhealth.com/sprained-foot-7506255)[1](https://www.verywellhealth.com/sprained-foot-7506255)[3](https://www.nationwidechildrens.org/conditions/foot-sprain-or-strain).
5.  [**Therapeutic exercises**: Strengthen and stretch the muscles surrounding the affected joint](https://www.verywellhealth.com/sprained-foot-7506255)[4](https://share.upmc.com/2014/03/physical-therapy-for-sprains-and-strains/).
6.  [**Joint mobilization techniques**: Performed by a physical therapist to the affected joint](https://www.verywellhealth.com/sprained-foot-7506255)[4](https://share.upmc.com/2014/03/physical-therapy-for-sprains-and-strains/).
7.  [**Heat, ice, and/or electrical stimulation**: These can be used to minimize swelling and pain and promote function](https://share.upmc.com/2014/03/physical-therapy-for-sprains-and-strains/)[4](https://share.upmc.com/2014/03/physical-therapy-for-sprains-and-strains/).

\=====================================

Any ideas of what would Daniel David Palmer or chiropractor trained by him do when trying to help?

Searching for: Daniel David Palmer chiropractic approach for chronic sprains and strains in feet

[Daniel David Palmer, the founder of chiropractic, believed that the human body had an ample supply of natural healing power transmitted through the nervous system](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Daniel_David_Palmer)[1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Daniel_David_Palmer). [**He suggested that if any one organ was affected by an illness, it merely must not be receiving its normal “nerve supply” which he dubbed a “spinal misalignment”, or subluxation**](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Daniel_David_Palmer)**[1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Daniel_David_Palmer).** [He saw chiropractic as a form of realigning to reestablish the supply](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Daniel_David_Palmer)[1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Daniel_David_Palmer).
[Palmer developed spinal manipulations to correct subluxations and thus unblock the flow of the innate](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Daniel_David_Palmer)[2](https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/978-3-030-53118-8_2). [He once quoted, "I am not the first person to replace subluxated vertebrae, but I do claim to be the first person to replace a displaced vertebrae by using the spinous and transverse processes as levers…and to develop the philosophy and science of chiropractic adjustments."](https://nationalacademyofosteopathy.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/12/manual_therapies_osteopathy__chiropractic.pdf)[3](https://nationalacademyofosteopathy.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/12/manual_therapies_osteopathy__chiropractic.pdf)
[The Palmer method involves combining several different techniques for more efficient healing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Daniel_David_Palmer)[4](https://medium.com/@PetrakChiro/the-palmer-method-of-chiropractic-d61893818137). [**One of these techniques is the Gonstead method, which is centered around the idea that the body’s foundation is the pelvic girdle**](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Daniel_David_Palmer)**[4](https://medium.com/@PetrakChiro/the-palmer-method-of-chiropractic-d61893818137).**
However, it’s important to note that while chiropractic care can help with a variety of conditions, it may not be the best or only treatment for your chronic sprains/strains and stiffness in your feet.

\===========================
I doubt if my chiropractor was even trained in Gonstead method.
He is the best I was able to arrange.
But has not been schooled in Palmer's School.
I will ask him anyway. He is the best I was able to arrange.
And he got my spine out from surgeons knife.

Few months ago, I did come across (I thought different chiropractic method). Sounded much different than what my chiropractors do.
It was a person from Australia discussing it.
Not sure If I could find him again.

Another idea.
[@DrEttinger](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/22307/) (chiropractor, but not only) offers 15 minutes free consultation. Call him, ask for suggestions.
Even a whole consultation is not going to break your bank.
\====================

**What is the Gonstead method?**

The Gonstead method is a system of chiropractic analysis and adjustment developed by Clarence S. [Gonstead](https://concisechiropractic.com/the-gonstead-method/)[1](https://concisechiropractic.com/the-gonstead-method/). [It aims to be as specific, precise, and accurate as possible, addressing only the problem areas (areas of subluxation) in the spine](https://concisechiropractic.com/the-gonstead-method/)[1](https://concisechiropractic.com/the-gonstead-method/). Here are the main elements of the Gonstead method:

1.  [**X-Ray Analysis**: Before treatment, x-rays are taken to identify exactly where your problem areas are and to see any possible abnormalities like fractures, arthritis, or degeneration](https://concisechiropractic.com/the-gonstead-method/)[2](https://blog.nhregister.com/lifestyle/2020/02/17/what-is-the-gonstead-technique-in-chiropractic-care/).
2.  [**Static Palpitation**: The chiropractor feels your spine while you’re in a stationary position to detect any heat, swelling, or tenderness](https://concisechiropractic.com/the-gonstead-method/)[2](https://blog.nhregister.com/lifestyle/2020/02/17/what-is-the-gonstead-technique-in-chiropractic-care/).
3.  [**Motion Palpitation**: The chiropractor feels your spine while you’re in motion to see how the vertebrae respond to each movement](https://concisechiropractic.com/the-gonstead-method/)[2](https://blog.nhregister.com/lifestyle/2020/02/17/what-is-the-gonstead-technique-in-chiropractic-care/).
4.  [**Nervoscope Instrumentation**: A Nervoscope is used to find uneven distributions of heat along your spine, which can indicate nerve pressure or inflammation](https://concisechiropractic.com/the-gonstead-method/)[2](https://blog.nhregister.com/lifestyle/2020/02/17/what-is-the-gonstead-technique-in-chiropractic-care/).
5.  [**Visualization**: The chiropractor uses all the other findings to evaluate the way you stand, sit, and move](https://concisechiropractic.com/the-gonstead-method/)[2](https://blog.nhregister.com/lifestyle/2020/02/17/what-is-the-gonstead-technique-in-chiropractic-care/).

[The Gonstead method begins with a basic biomechanical principle of physics, **viewing the pelvic girdle as the body’s foundation**](https://concisechiropractic.com/the-gonstead-method/)[1](https://concisechiropractic.com/the-gonstead-method/). [Any slight change or shift in the foundation can cause a great amount of deviation in the top part of the structure and, perhaps, ultimately, its collapse](https://concisechiropractic.com/the-gonstead-method/)[1](https://concisechiropractic.com/the-gonstead-method/). [Similarly, the Gonstead method looks to the pelvic girdle as the body’s foundation](https://concisechiropractic.com/the-gonstead-method/)[1](https://concisechiropractic.com/the-gonstead-method/).
[This method is considered the “Gold Standard” of chiropractic techniques because it aims to be as specific as possible, unlike many other chiropractic methods that are more generalized](https://blog.nhregister.com/lifestyle/2020/02/17/what-is-the-gonstead-technique-in-chiropractic-care/)[2](https://blog.nhregister.com/lifestyle/2020/02/17/what-is-the-gonstead-technique-in-chiropractic-care/). [However, mastering the art of delivering a specific adjustment takes a lot of practice and dedication](https://concisechiropractic.com/the-gonstead-method/)[1](https://concisechiropractic.com/the-gonstead-method/).

\========

Spectracel.com
For these tests you must give blood sample within USA. So rather look for something similar in your neighborhood.

DUTCHtest.com

### [DUTCH Plus® - DUTCH Test](https://dutchtest.com/product/dutch-plus/)

The DUTCH Plus® takes hormone testing to a new level. In addition to sex hormones and their metabolites, the DUTCH Complete™ looks at the overall diurnal pattern of free cortisol, and the total and distribution of cortisol metabolites. The DUTCH Plus® adds the Cortisol Awakening Response (CAR)...

 dutchtest.com

This test will work anywhere. Samples are mailed.

When thinking of those tests, think of brain or nerve surgeries (without scalpel).
When I look at my test results, I am eventually attempting to restore my values to values that 20-25 yo healthy man would have.
I am aiming there even when I am actually 84yo.

His response was that he would initially look into
C1 & C2
and eventually into moving cranial bones some.

Mostly it is a process of discovery what works.
So, it takes some time to figure it out,
assuming that it is successful,
long process of maintenance.

### Anthony Smith — 2024-01-22T20:34:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-going-to-heal-my-feet.28502/post-330476>

> [NicklasK said:](/goto/post?id=330403)
>
> My main problem in one line:
>
> Chronic sprains/strains and stiffness in my feet that relapses when I try to gradually rehabilitate them.

Years ago my wife had to spend about 2 years wearing a boot because of hairline fractures in her feet. For the pain the foot Dr. would give her steroid shots in her feet every few months. After 2 years the Dr said no more shots the next thing to do was cut the nerves in her feet!

We had past experience with Chiropractic, but one bad experience had kept us away from that for several years. Faced with the foot Dr’s “option” before cutting nerves we consulted a different local Chiropractor. We were told her neck was out throwing her hips out which was affecting her posture and weight balance which was causing the foot problems. She had 3 treatments and within a few weeks was out of the boot, out of pain and never had any more issues.

Every situation is different but sometimes there IS a simple solution (if you can find it). Hope this helps.

### NicklasK — 2024-01-23T07:14:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-going-to-heal-my-feet.28502/post-330495>

\---
JanSz:
\---

Ok, I'll put the chiropractic on my list on things to try out. I'm not sure if I can find anyone in my area who is good enough at it, the one I tried last didn't seem very convincing to say the least.

I've tried a lot of therapists during the first few years after I stoped being able to walk, and was very disappointed. Massage, chiropracic, kinesiology, doctors of course, physical therapists. One of the chiropractors I tried was supposed to do an x-ray of my neck, but then he refused to do that for strange reasons even though we had already agreed on it, and that definitely didn't improve my impression.

I also got a massage education many years ago, so I have a lot of experience working on my own body and others'.

I bought a self-therapy program called "block therapy", where you use 2 special wood blocks to work on the fascia system of the body, to get it to release and stretch. It seems promising.

Thanks for asking your chiropractor - I do believe that a good chiropractor could help, I just need to actually find one that is good enough.

\---
pfedorow:
\---

Yes, I've been working on the EMF in my environment for a long time. I reacted badly to computer screens, but I found out that if I used a projector instead, and put that projector in a metal box with just a small hole in front, where the light could get out, it was much better. However, the picture was not sharp enough for me to read text on it easily.

I then bought a 65" OLED TV and placed it 3 m away from my body, and that works even better. It seems that if there's enough distance between my body and the screen, the problem is greatly reduced.

I also used an optical USB cable to achieve galvanic isolation between my body and all the electrical noise being carried on the regular USB line of the computer. The remote end was powered by the computer USB, and the end near my body was powered by a USB battery bank. That cable stopped working when I got a new motherboard, so I have a new one in the mail.

I have an incandescent light bulb to light the room, and I'm using a battery powered NIR light part of the time.

One main difficulty is that I'm not going to be able to get completely away from the 230V AC cables in the wall, and those can be problematic as well. However, some years ago I spent some months in an environment where I was not close to any of those wires, and I didn't get any significant improvements from that.

I'm also not looking at screens after 6 pm, going to bed at 9 pm, and also wearing blue blockers between 6 and 9 pm, so that I'm getting a break from the vast majority of the artifical light the last 3 hours before bed, and that seems to help me sleep much better and have better energy.

Did you do anything that I have not mentioned above to fix your environment?

\---
Anthony Smith:
\---

Amazing that it worked so well for her! So yes, I agree that it might be a good idea to try again to find a good chiropractor.

### pfedorow — 2024-01-23T20:55:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-going-to-heal-my-feet.28502/post-330514>

> [Anthony Smith said:](/goto/post?id=330217)
>
> I have wondered about Oura rings and nnEMF myself and this post prompted me to look that up. There are several YouTubes on this but I link to this one because she uses the better quality RF meter (Safe & Sound Pro II) than the other reviewers.

I know RF is low in airplane mode but you still have a small powered device on your body during your sleep cycle. How do you measure that? Do you need a precise body voltage to read that? I meet many people obsessed with these sleep scores on how they feel for the day, but how are these results measured from a quantum lifestyle perspective? It seems counter intuitive to do jacks work and rely on big tech to tell you how you feel when they most likely do not ever have the big picture of everything.

### Anthony Smith — 2024-01-24T05:43:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-going-to-heal-my-feet.28502/post-330524>

> [pfedorow said:](/goto/post?id=330514)
>
> I know RF is low in airplane mode but you still have a small powered device on your body during your sleep cycle. How do you measure that? Do you need a precise body voltage to read that? I meet many people obsessed with these sleep scores on how they feel for the day, but how are these results measured from a quantum lifestyle perspective? It seems counter intuitive to do jacks work and rely on big tech to tell you how you feel when they most likely do not ever have the big picture of everything.

Big difference between 60Hz (50Hz) AC powered devices on or close to the body and DC (battery) power devices. Our bodies are DC not AC. If there are problems and issues measuring can be a fast track to a solution even if there is a (small) negative to the measuring process.

### NicklasK — 2024-01-24T16:11:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-going-to-heal-my-feet.28502/post-330528>

\---
pfedorow:
\---

I haven't gone camping, but I have been in a location where my body voltage meter showed that I was below the detection limit, and the other meters also showed nothing.

I will go to my family's vacation home at the beach this summer and get as low nnEMF as possible, and as much sunlight and swimming (I can swim just using my arms) in the ocean as possible.

I had the best guy for measuring building nnEMF that I could find some years ago, and he said he didn't find anything alarming. I also bought some meters for testing radio frequency, AC electric, AC magnetic, dirty electricity and body voltage.

Regarding the Oura, I have measured it and found nothing with my meters, when it is on flight mode, and I haven't noticed anything negative after starting to use it. For now, I have concluded that the benefit of being able to more clearly detect what gives me better sleep and HRV are worth more than any negative effects, but at some point I will probably stop wearing it daily.

potassium is important in the transmission of nerve impulses in your brain, spinal cord and peripheral nervous system.
Nerve impulses transmitting information from one nerve to the next happens as the result of electrical activity.
\=================
You may want to get test

potassium RBC

to see where your potassium is

or get Potassium Bicarbonate
take one or two flat teaspoons daily for 3-4 days
and see if that changes anything in how your feet react.

### JanSz — 2024-01-28T16:14:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-going-to-heal-my-feet.28502/post-330670>

[@NicklasK](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/30245/)

Are you (your feet) possibly affected by some situations listed below?
Reading the below, I was especially thinking about some dental work that could have affected positions of your cranial bones.

Remember, my chiropractor, in regard to your feet problem, he thought of adjusting C1, C2 and moving your cranial bones.
\=================
Have you checked a pressure in your eyeballs?

This pressure check is called tonometry. In the past, you may have had an eye pressure test using a puff of air. Now most ophthalmologists use a more accurate device that measures pressure by direct contact with the eye.

Normal eye pressure ranges from 12-22 mm Hg.
Find out this number. Do that yearly.
If gets too close to 22 it is time to act.

\=================
Your body is naturally asymmetrical.
Balance is maintained through the integration of these system imbalances.
When balanced integration fails, structural weaknesses and pain can develop.
\---
While walking barefoot outdoors on uneven terrain is a great way to ground, walking barefoot on hard, flat surfaces, such as tile or concrete, can cause problems, including bunions.
\---
Things like eyeglasses, LASIK surgery, dental implants and root canals can affect your sensory experience, and hence your gait and posture, which in turn can result in pain syndromes.
\---
Dental work can affect your bite, which affects your cranial and cervical posture.
If teeth are pulled and not replaced with an implant, your brain may not be able to determine where your center of gravity is, causing your body to overcompensate to one side or the other.
\----
LASIK eye surgery can trigger unexpected problems.
One of the main problems is that if it fixes you into a set prescription that is wrong, it can lock in dysfunction.
With glasses, you’re not locked into one prescription.
You can change it and get new glasses if it turns out that your symmetry was off.

/////////

\====================
Human Photosynthesis : the real source of ATP

[Human Photosynthesis : the real source of ATP - Optimumosteo](https://optimumosteo.com/en/human-photosynthesis-atp/)

### [Human Photosynthesis : the real source of ATP - Optimumosteo](https://optimumosteo.com/en/human-photosynthesis-atp/)

In a recent podcast with Sawyer Ji, a researcher and founder of the online encyclopedia on natural medicine Greenmedinfo.com, he revealed the beginnings in

 optimumosteo.com

Human Photosynthesis : the real source of ATP

[Leave a Comment](https://optimumosteo.com/en/human-photosynthesis-atp/#respond) / [Non classé](https://optimumosteo.com/en/category/non-classe-en/) / By [Janeck Olczyk](https://optimumosteo.com/en/author/janecko/)

**Human Photosynthesis : the real source of ATP**

In a recent podcast with Sawyer Ji, a researcher and founder of the online encyclopedia on natural medicine Greenmedinfo.com, he revealed the beginnings in the field of understanding photosynthesis in mammals and therefore, in humans. Indeed, this concept, which seems wrong when joining these two words together and which until recently was attributed to the kingdom of plants, is beginning to be understood in the human organism for the production of ATP at the cellular level. In osteopathy, we always seek to increase mobility in order to optimize the functioning of the body. We will see later how this can be done by addressing body fluids

**How does human photosynthesis works?**

The concept of human photosynthesis goes hand in hand with the understanding of melanin, this skin pigment, which defines the color palette present in it. Studies of sea turtles that can hibernate underwater for 4 or 5 months have shown that melanin can dissociate the molecules of water to allow this reptile to breathe underwater. Dr. Arturo Solis, who studies this amazing ability of melanin explains that the energy source behind the production of ATP is not glucose but the energy stored in the hydrogen which must be separated from the oxygen to give the precious electrons to the mitochondria so that they produce ATP. Mitochondria are the organelles inside cells that are the ATP factories and hold most of the electrons in their transport chain through the supply of the hydrogen electron separated by melanin. But the separation of the water contained in the body is not an easy task because it is normally necessary to heat the H2O to 2000 degrees Celsius in order to separate the gaseous components. Melanin does this because it captures photons from sunlight and holds a phenomenal amount of energy.[\[1\]](https://optimumosteo.com/en/human-photosynthesis-atp/#_ftn1)

It’s the miracle of nature. The theory behind which the human body works by burning glucose and oxidizing it comes from Lawassie and Presley over 300 years ago. It seems to be lacking proof in several places and remains only a well accepted theory that is the basis of human biology. Recent discoveries challenge this once again and lead to a completely different explanation, according to Professor Daniel Nocera of MIT: energy comes from the dissociation of water, stored in hydrogen atoms. Hydrogen is the perfect candidate; it’s the main energy conveyor throughout the universe. And melanin is everywhere in the body, and in the environment. Astronomers can even capture huge clouds of melanin molecules in space, the size of a galaxy, in pictures. Phenomenal!

**The miracle action of melanin**

Melanin is even stronger than chlorophyll in plants to do the same thing. In plants, once the water is separated by chlorophyll, they expel oxygen, since it is toxic. In mammals, melanin dissociates two molecules of water, it captures 4 electrons for the electron transport chain, and then re-forms the water molecule. The process by which melanin produces this mechanism is on the order of nanos or picoseconds. So almost an instantaneous continuum. And melanin is everywhere.

Glucose, fat, proteins and metabolites transported by the blood, build and reconstruct matter, the body. But the energy used for their transformation comes from the dissociation of water, and ultimately, from the sun.

This is what the melanin produced in the eyes and the optic nerve of the embryo at five months is for. Certainly not to see, but to capture photons of light through the mother’s abdomen and produce ATP for the high energy demands of the embryo’s genesis.

The place with the most melanin in the brain is the substancia nigra and the locus cerebellum. So are the choroid plexuses of the ventricles which can extract energy from the water in the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF)[\[2\]](https://optimumosteo.com/en/human-photosynthesis-atp/#_ftn2). In osteopathy we use craniosacral techniques to mobilize and optimize the circulation of the CSF. Dr. Solis explains that when these places in the brain lose their efficiency, energy production becomes deficient and the appearance of diseases like Alzheimer’s occurs. A whole new branch of medicine is opening up to find new solutions with these discoveries. And in osteopathy, the mobility of fluids, perhaps to optimize the dissociation of water and human photosynthesis, has always been paramount in his overall vision of the body.

According to the interview with Sawyer Ji, there are compounds that are particularly rich in bioassimilable melanin in nature in order to increase our melanin and thus increase the production of ATP so that cells can have more energy for all their functions. Of all the natural sources, usually very colorful foods, the richest one would be this well known fungus which grows in the boreal forests of the northern hemisphere, **Chaga.** Its dark color would testify to this. And a second compound proposed by Ben Greenfield, author, athlete and popular health coach names **methylene blue** as a compound ultra rich in melanin, hence its color once again dark blue. A pharmaceutical grade source must be found, but this compound is already known as a powerful nootropic, precisely because it can increase ATP production under the influence of light. These two substances, chaga and methylene blue would be more effective under exposure to natural sunlight or under photo-bio-modulation technologies with infrared lamps.

The most astonishing thing, get ready, would be the ability of melanin to use not only visible light to dissociate water and aid energy production, but the whole wave spectrum from radio waves to gamma rays. It could be that melanin can therefore use the waves emitted by unnatural EMFs like wifi, 3 and 4G, 5G etc. It remains to be confirmed if the addition of exogenous melanin could really protect us from the toxic technological environment in which we bathe. My article on the [protection of non-natural EMFs and 5G](https://optimumosteo.com/emfs-and-5g-protection-part-1/) makes an excellent summary of the studies, danger and ways to protect yourself, which you can read by clicking [here](https://optimumosteo.com/emfs-and-5g-protection-part-1/).

[\[1\]](https://optimumosteo.com/en/human-photosynthesis-atp/#_ftnref1) [https://embajadamundialdeactivistasporlapaz.com/en/press/dr-arturo-solis-human-photosynthesis](https://embajadamundialdeactivistasporlapaz.com/en/press/dr-arturo-solis-human-photosynthesis)

[\[2\]](https://optimumosteo.com/en/human-photosynthesis-atp/#_ftnref2) [https://www.hilarispublisher.com/op...t-of-alzheimers-disease-1948-593X.1000079.pdf](https://www.hilarispublisher.com/open-access/human-photosynthesis-a-turning-point-in-the-understanding-and-treatment-of-alzheimers-disease-1948-593X.1000079.pdf)

### JanSz — 2024-01-31T23:00:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-going-to-heal-my-feet.28502/post-330801>

open source Parkinson's **does it sometime affects feet movements**

Parkinson’s disease is a neurodegenerative disorder that can cause a range of symptoms, including difficulty with movement and coordination.
[One of the most common symptoms of Parkinson’s disease is **gait abnormalities**, which can include problems with walking and poor balance](https://www.parkinson.org/understanding-parkinsons/movement-symptoms/trouble-moving)[1](https://www.parkinson.org/understanding-parkinsons/movement-symptoms/trouble-moving).
Feet begin to shuffle and performing two tasks at once becomes more difficult.
[Turning becomes challenging, often leading to a freezing episode and sometimes a fall](https://www.parkinson.org/understanding-parkinsons/movement-symptoms/trouble-moving)[1](https://www.parkinson.org/understanding-parkinsons/movement-symptoms/trouble-moving).

### JanSz — 2024-01-31T23:06:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-going-to-heal-my-feet.28502/post-330802>

May this is gait abnormalities last for 10 years relatively unchanged?

Searching for: Parkinson's disease gait abnormalities progression

[Parkinson’s disease is a progressive disorder, and symptoms such as gait abnormalities typically worsen over time](https://www.frontiersin.org/articles/10.3389/fnagi.2020.577435/full)[1](https://www.frontiersin.org/articles/10.3389/fnagi.2020.577435/full)[2](https://www.physio-pedia.com/Gait_Re-education_in_Parkinson%27s).

[However, the rate of progression can vary significantly from person to person](https://www.frontiersin.org/articles/10.3389/fnagi.2020.577435/full)[1](https://www.frontiersin.org/articles/10.3389/fnagi.2020.577435/full)[2](https://www.physio-pedia.com/Gait_Re-education_in_Parkinson%27s).
[A study observed gait progression over 6 years in Parkinson’s disease and found that discrete gait impairments continue to progress, reflecting a combination of disease-specific progression and age-related change](https://www.frontiersin.org/articles/10.3389/fnagi.2020.577435/full)[1](https://www.frontiersin.org/articles/10.3389/fnagi.2020.577435/full).

[Gait changes were mostly unrelated to dopaminergic medication adjustments, highlighting limitations of current dopaminergic therapy and the need to improve interventions targeting gait decline](https://www.frontiersin.org/articles/10.3389/fnagi.2020.577435/full)[1](https://www.frontiersin.org/articles/10.3389/fnagi.2020.577435/full).

That being said, it’s theoretically possible for some individuals to experience relatively stable symptoms over a period of 10 years, especially with appropriate management and treatment. [**However, this would be considered unusual**](https://www.healthline.com/health/parkinsons/gait)**.**
