> I'm a 41 year old man living in Denmark. For 10 years, I have been crawling on my knees and using a wheelchair because my feet have a neurological problem.
>
> it started after years of stressful work doing undergraduate, graduate and PhD work in nanoscience, including a serious concussion in a car crash, which I was however able to mostly heal. I was also doing standard dancing at a competition level, as well as weight lifting - not to become bodybuilder-muscular, but to get athletically muscular.
>
> I've been through the medical system, gotten a MRI scan and x-rays, and the closest thing I got to a diagnosis was CRPS (complex regional pain syndrome). Basically my feet are extremely easily sprained/strained, and I have been unable to train them to a level where I can start walking again, without the sprain/strain recurring.
>
> I'm doing the following things (plus a bunch more), most of which are from Jack's recommendations:
>
> \- eating a lot of wild-caught fish, mostly canned, especially sardines, cod roe, cod liver, and also oysters a couple of times a month
> \- eating mostly home-grown vegetables such as onions, cabbage, swiss chard, green beans
> \- eating seaweed, mostly nori, but also some arame, and trying out various of the types Jack recommends
> \- I get 5 minutes of bright light soon after waking at 4-6 am, however this is with a Beurer light therapy device which is the best I can do at the moment since the sun doesn't rise until 9.30 am at this time of year
> \- I get outside and watch the sunrise for 5-10 minutes, and if the water isn't frozen, I dip my head in a bowl of water, and then do as many knee-squats as I can
> \- I watch the sunset, usually with my shirt off, and do some more knee squats
> \- I don't look at screens after 6 pm, I start wearing blue blockers ad 7 pm, and I go to bed at 9 pm
> \- I grow and drink wheatgrass juice, this has helped significantly with my energy and skin issues (I previously had acne)
> \- I use a "block blue light" red light device for 20 minutes a day
> \- I massage my entire body with foam rollers and have just started doing the "block therapy" program which is based on the same principle
> \- I started eating 1-4 tsp of sea salt a day
>
> Things are improving somewhat with my feet, but it still feels like I have to be very careful with how I use them.
>
> Am I missing any low-hanging fruit? (seasonally and climate appropriate fruit, of course)
>
> Or even higher-hanging fruit would be great as well, if it has a good chance at healing me.
>
> I'm also considering a Sperti Fiji light, as I've read that some people here recommend them.
>
> I'm still getting too much artificial light, however I have set my computer screen at 3000K, and using incandescent or 2700K LED light bulbs around the house. I'm working on improving this area - that is, getting more natural sunlight and less artificial.

Hi NicklasK,

[@JanSz](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/933/) gave me a head’s up about your story and journey. Since knowing and learning about Jack’s work, have you seen any progress ? Significantly ?

I’m a masseuse with some form of EHS and the ability to actually feel energy. All sorts and all kinds. Check my journey, if you’d like, it’s a 4 part ride. I haven’t followed any courses or followed any guidelines, I have always stayed far away from doctors, medical therapists and especially physical therapists. No offense to those making a living off of those jobs.

I just memorized human anatomy and its fascial systems 4 years ago and just started. It always been off feel and deep tissue. Very deep. My massages have been systemic, extensive and relatively painful but with the right type of breathing it’s perfectly doable. My absolute focus is on fascia and all meridians making up for the body’s inner & outer cast. Muscle almost never comes into play. It

has always been just me, my hands and an occupied table. Door or windows ajar to let some sun in, blocking window glass to have minimal blue light exposure. And I’ve been using blue blocking glasses myself and on my clients during massages in the last 6 / 7 months combined with an occasional red light Firestorm. Massages range from 1 to 3hrs with extreme cases longer than that. I’ve done about about 500 - 600 massages in the last couple of years with nothing but great succes. My last massage has been in the Flemish Ardennes, indoors, fireplace burning bright for some nice IR, windows wide open and me and my client both wearing blue blocking glasses. Spontaneous, unplanned and it turned out to be one of the best on many levels. Massage lasted for 2 1/2 hrs with a great, emotional and therefore rewarding result. Client was a 22 y.o. boy who ate like shit in an equally shitty environment. He had deep gut issues, nausea, severe problems with going to the bathroom, headaches, pains and aches in his lower back especially during driving, scolioses and loss of sleep. All problems went away and afterwards even his mood went up significantly. Ofcourse I didn’t solve his scoliosis but he has got no pain, regular daily bathroom visits and his bowels are ok.

I have never come across a case like yours but I’m trying to give you some sort of a reference.

This is what I told Jansz upon hearing about you :
‘My guess would be his gut & inner column, starting with the deep gut. Everything from his deep gut/lower spine up and along his diaphragm, intercostals, scm’s, occipital area and skull combined with deep lower gut, inguinal ligaments, lower spine/coccyx, pelvis and adductors, hamstrings down to his soleuses and plantair fascia. Definitely would be a 2 / 3 hrs session if he was my client’

I would start by looking at the places you are losing most energy and the shape and rotation in both your eyesockets. That would tell me a lot about your brain, skull, spine and gut.

I would have loved to work with and on you but we might run into a geographical problem., as I’m living in the Netherlands at the moment.

Have you tried a deep tissue massage before ? For this extended period of time ?

I still think some strong IR and UV, with a tight light/dark cycle would be the ultimate solution

Tell me what you think. .

### NicklasK — 2024-02-19T19:02:59+0200

Leonaero\_Light:
\---

Thank you very much! I haven't focused on finding a good body therapist for a long time, because I had very low success with it previously. I also have a basic massage therapy education, and back before my feet started getting problems, I also had very low success finding other massage therapists that didn't just do very superficial treatments. However, I believe from your description that there would be a good chance that a therapist like you could make a significant difference for me.

Since starting a bunch of things from Jack's recommendations, I have gotten stronger, I have more robust energy, and my feet and hands are a bit better some of the time - however, no breakthroughs in those areas yet. I am far from implementing all of his main points to the maximum level, though, but I'm working on it.

I've started doing 2x10 minutes of red+IR light on my legs and feet daily, and I received a Sperti Fiji UV light (seems to be popular on this forum) a week ago that I've also started using. I'm dipping my head and hands in cold water outside once a day most days, and preparing to increase the cold therapy. I'm eating a lot of fish, especially sardines, cod liver and wild-caught salmon. Also adding seaweed to many meals. I have added a significant number of the supplements that Jack recommends for peripheral neuropathy, such as GLA-rich evening primrose oil and resveratrol.

So, I'd say that I'm not too far from being able to say that I have gotten significantly better since discovering Jack, but I'm not quite there yet.

I believe that you are spot on about the gut and the lower spine, and also the rest of the description.

The geographical situation is a shame - you're just barely far enough away that it would be pretty complicated to visit you.

So for now, I think I will start by seeing if I can find a good enough local therapist and talk to him/her about your suggestions, and maybe at some point it would be realistic for me to get a treatment from you.

Yes, I've considered that. With my experience of my situation, I believe there's a significant likelihood that it will take much more than a few sessions before I'm healed to the point that I can walk again, so it would be something where I could see doing it for at least 3 months, probably longer. And I don't quite see that as a main option right now, especially with all the new things I have recently added to my program. For instance, my feet are doing better than they have in a while, though there's still a very long way to go.

Also, I have resistance towards the idea that I would have to go away from my ancestral soil in order to heal. I know that Jack recommends doing that, to get much more natural EMF, but I am not convinced that it would be necessary.

About DDW - you wrote that you had been drinking it for a long time and have had various testing done, have you noticed any improvements that you attribute to the DDW?

I have bought some 25 ppm DDW, but it tastes pretty bad, so I tested its conductivity and it's at 650 µS, while my hard tap water is at 550 µS. This surprised me, as I assumed that they were all using fractional distillation to make DDW, and if they do, then why would they add bad-tasting stuff afterwards? So I've started distilling it to solve the problem.

Discussions about ancestral soil can be compared to Jack's discussing haplotypes.
I got interested in that and got tested at
23andme.com
My case turned out to be very easy.
My haplotype is H5
and my 10000 years ago Grandma lived in the area where Tbilisi Georgia is today.
I think from Jack's discussions it came that the most important part of attempting to replicate that location is sunlight exposure. That can be done by living in places of the same latitude.
Living in Poland I was too much up North.
But when 53 years ago, I immigrated to NJ USA 07054, now I am tiny bit south of Tbilisi.
I must say I feel somewhat better sun exposure vise in my present place.
But more important, air in Poland was terrible.
My sinuses problems disappeared immediately in USA, (but they are still in my memory).
I also have good tropical experience.
Slightly over one year in Itaorna Brazill (build Nuclear Power station there).
After 3-4 months I was uncomfortable with equal day and night lengths.
Absolutely no problem with sunlight there, hardly seeing my shadow.
Living in the house right on the beach with my family.

So, with your idea of ancestral soil, that I support, you may want to get closer idea of your ancestry by finding your haplotype.

Jack talks mostly about mother's haplotype because of mitochondria.

My father seems to be from more local but also slightly southern places to Poland.
Additionally, I have also found some of my father's relatives.
Which proved to me that my father is actually my biological father.
Both of my parents had terrible life in Communist Poland (mostly hunger) (and getting to prison)
Additionally, instead of using me as a help on their farm they send me to my moms' sister in big city so I could get to high school and college.

\---
Chlo\_e:
\---

I've spent a long time and done lots of experiments as to what kinds and amounts of radiofrequency and general nnEMF exposure give me detectable problems. I'm downloading the data from my Oura ring for 1 minute during the morning, with my ring off the finger and my body 2 meters away from the ring while it talks to my phone. The rest of the day, both are on flight mode. My Cornet rf meter has shown below the detection limit for almost the entire last 10 years, only recently some is showing up some of the time.

Everything is hardwired, and we don't use WiFi. We are 50 m (150 ft) away from our nearest neighbor.

I have also written earlier in this thread about more observations I've made regarding nnEMF exposure.

So at this point, I don't believe that the amount of rf radiation I'm getting is anywhere near the top of my concerns.

\---
JanSz:
\---

I've been to various warmer countries, and I almost always get sluggish and in a bad mood from the heat, even if it's not too much warmer than Denmark. I can even have trouble when we have a warmer than usual summer here. I really enjoy it when the first cool days of autumn arrive. I haven't tested my genetics, mainly because I don't want to expose my genetic information too much, as it'll all get hacked and spread on the internet eventually, if it's on 23andme or other companies' servers.

My mother is as scandinavian as can be, very blonde and 6 feet tall, and I am 6'4", so that's enough for me to conclude that I'll probably do best at my current latitude.

Disappointing that you have not noticed anything from drinking DDW. I will give it a shot for a while.

Sounds like you have been very diligent with the RF monitoring. I suppose, is there anything either physical or habitual that you have done very regularly and never had a break from, even if it is using screens when they are hard-wired and in flight mode? It wasn't until I bought a cheap AM radio that I could appreciate the amount of EMI still coming off screens even when they are not plugged into anything at all. I read you use a separate screen with fibre optic cable. But I have also spoken to people who have needed to take breaks from all technology including hard-wired use for periods of time to recover from certain ailments. Unless you are being exposed to something else in your environment and presumably have ruled out severe nutrient deficiencies during the course of this problem? You mentioned the symptoms started after significant periods of study and work in nanoscience, were you exposed to anything during that time which could have caused damage, other than likely long hours behind a computer? It is possible that there is a problem with nerve/soft tissue turnover from an issue higher up as opposed to the issue originating in the feet. For what it's worth, I drank 25ppm DDW for 6 months with no discernible benefit, but I hear of others who it has helped.

I also wanted to ask how you use the fibre optic USB cable? What does that allow you to connect between/do? I now also use a fibre optic cable to separate my laptop from the mains, but I use a DC powered media converter to facilitate that. I don't know much about the fibre-USB cables and how they are useful if you wouldn't mind sharing?

### NicklasK — 2024-02-27T17:07:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-going-to-heal-my-feet.28502/post-331996>

\---
Chlo\_e:
\---

No, there's nothing habitual nnEMF-wise that I am aware of that I haven't had a break from. I've measured radiofrequency, electrical fields, magnetic fields, dirty electricity (Stetzerizer microsurge meter and AM radio) and body voltage (sensitive voltmeter).

The AM radio is one of the reasons that I'm using a 65" monitor that's 3 m away from my body. The radio picks up noise about 30-50 cm from the monitor, so I'm using another 2.5 m as extra buffer. This is the setup that has resulted in the lowest amount of stress to my body by far. Before this, I also tried a monitor that was custom built (modified from a commercial monitor) by a very EMF sensitive electrical engineer. That monitor gave me significant symptoms. I tried a projector that was in a metal box with a small hole for the light to come out of - this worked well symptom-wise, but it was sound-noisy and the picture was not sharp enough.

I considered using an ultra short throw projector in a metal box, but I don't have enough space for that.

The screen does not have an optic fibre cable option, it uses HDMI, so I'm relying on distance there.

The fibre optic cable that I'm using is a USB extender, so that the electric noise from the computer power supply and the mains cannot "seep" into my keyboard, mouse and USB sound card and radiate into my body. The end near the computer is powered by the USB port, and the end near me is powered by a USB power bank. The signal gets converted first om electric to optic, sent through the optic cable, and at the other end, the signal gets converted from light into electric, but without the noise that may have been present.

This is the product I'm using:

### [USB2.0 Optical Transceiver USB to Optical Transceiver Optical Fiber Extender | eBay](https://www.ebay.com/itm/363807506282)

USB2.0 Optical Transceiver USB to Optical Transceiver USB2.0 Optical Fiber Extender Transmission and Transceiver Extension 20km. The USB20 fiber optic extender consists of two parts: receiver and transmitter.

www.ebay.com

For a while, I also used an optic ethernet converter (I assume you mean ethernet by "DC powered media converter"?) to stop the noise from that source, but I decided that it wasn't worth it. I'm now relying on distance to solve that part as well.

I have ruled out the easy-to-detect nutritional deficiencies, by blood test and by hair mineral analysis (not the simplistic type of hair analysis where they just recommend adding what's low on the analysis and avoiding that which is high, the body is much more complex than that). This does not mean that I'm not deficient in something, but if I am, it's probably not something obvious. Except, I've probably been deficient in natural sunlight for a long time. I'm working on solving that - by being outdoors and getting better at dealing with the cold, as well as using an IR+LED panel, and a Sperti Fiji UV lamp.

I believe that nnEMF avoidance is not at all enough for me to fully recover - I believe that I need a sufficiently low amount of nnEMF along with lots of natural sunlight, preferably lots of cold exposure, maybe regular sauna, and ocean bathing would be great as well. And grounding. And probably there's a bunch of other things that would be very helpful that I haven't found yet.

I've also started to implement many of Terry Wahls' recommendations. She is a doctor who effectively cured herself of MS, and I find her books very impressive. Also, Jack speaks highly of her. There's a lot of overlap between her program and Jack's recommendations.

My problems gradually increased over a number of years. My guess is that EMF from monitors, a tendency to stress, trying to do standard dancing at a high competitive level, doing weight lifting, eating more than my digestion could handle over a long period of time to try and gain weight as I was pretty skinny, and then the traumatic brain injury from the car crash I was in in 2011.

I also ate a lot of sushi over about a year during that time period, which could have given me significant amounts of heavy metals.

Yes, I believe that there might very well be issues higher up in the system, but the practitioners that I've tried haven't been able to cause any improvement in my symptoms. I'm currently doing block therapy (blocktherapy.com and there's a youtube channel by the same name).

This summer, I am going to my family's vacation home and aiming at swimming in the ocean and getting sun as much as possible. In the last 10 years, I have done very little of that as I didn't realize the power of the sun and the ocean to heal.

In the last 3 years, I've become very interested in growing food in my 750 m2 (7500 sq ft) garden, so that made me reluctant to go away for more than a few days during the season, as the plants needed watering, and the automated systems I tried didn't work very well. However, last autumn I switched over to the "Ruth Stout" system where you just cover the soil with thick hay, which dramatically reduces the watering requirements - which means that I can be away for much longer!

### NicklasK — 2024-03-03T18:50:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-going-to-heal-my-feet.28502/post-332163>

\---
JanSz:
\---

I definitely agree that concluding anything about carbohydrates as a group cab be higly misleading. For instance, for many years I did very poorly on all types of grains that I could find - wheat, rye, oats, barley, spelt, amaranth, quinoa, etc, but then I tried Hopi blue corn, and I've been eating about 80 lbs/year for the last 7 years without any problems. I even tried zero carb, or carnivore, as the current label is, for 7 months in 2009, which was catastrophic for me (extremely low energy).

I trusted the wrong people who said that the low energy would soon pass and that everything would be fine, then. Well, that didn't happen.

\---
pfedorow:
\---

Yes, that is mostly what I'm doing. The blue corn isn't seasonal for Denmark, of course, but it works way better for me than many of the things that are in season.

Many plants from the onion family and the cabbage grow nicely through the Danish winter, and carrots can also grow if you cover then with some hay/straw during the frost.

Ok, I will listen to it - where can I find it?

### NicklasK — 2024-03-07T19:29:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-going-to-heal-my-feet.28502/post-332286>

> [pfedorow said:](/goto/post?id=332283)
>
> Yes, occasionally, but circumstances are different. light stability, and hanging out on the beach would help him dramatically.
>
> I am not sure why no one called him out on his light therapy device for morning wake-up, as it seems like a terrible idea for his time zone as he should be resting not trying to live like a palm tree.
> also growing wheatgrass, which is probably grown with indoor lights, causing more inflammation.
>
> He should generally start with the basics and work his way up.

I was unsure about the morning full spectrum light, so I've used it on/off for a while. It's still not entirely clear to me if it brings significant benefit. You're saying I should be resting instead of trying to be a palm tree, but I was only using it after I woke up naturally without an alarm.

It did have the effect of making me very tired just before bedtime, which felt like probably a good thing.

I don't buy the idea that indoor wheatgrass would cause inflammation. On the contrary, it reduces it. There's a lot of science backing indoor grown wheatgrass for healing nasty diseases, and the main reason I'm using it is that it increases my energy, which has been very low much of the time for years. I recommend the book "Wheatgrass, nature's finest medicine" for an overview. It has also eliminated the small amount of acne I had.

So, some of the basics I'm doing:

Getting sunrise and sunset light, 5-15 minutes each (most days getting both)
Eating fish (mostly sardines, wild-caught salmon, cod liver, cod roe) most days, and sometimes seafood such as shrimp, also going to add regular oysters soon
Eating in-season vegetables (mostly home-grown) every day
Doing immersion of my head and hands into cold water in an outdoor tub, typically 30-60 seconds, every morning - it's been close to freezing most of the time during the last few months
Going to bed at 9 pm every day
Stopping the computer at 6 pm at the latest every day
Using blue blockers in the evening
Drinking DDW some of the time, still trying to see how much noticeable benefit it gives, sometimes it seems to give me more energy
Low omega-6
Eating wakame, arame or dulse daily
1.3 L of distilled water daily (my body reacts better to it than to RO)

And I'm also doing some things that Jack recommends in an article on peripheral neuropathy:
Evening primrose oil, 10 mL/day, because of the GLA content (I also found a very interesting scientific article of evening primrose oil for peripheral neuropathy in experimental animals)
resveratrol
carnosine
coconut
Plus I'm also working on adding more of the supplements he recommends in that article.

I realize I'm still getting too little sunlight, so I'm working on increasing the amount. It's already getting noticeably easier for me to tolerate the cold weather, so that makes getting more sunlight easier as well.

I haven't seen jack recommend it, but I'm also supplementing with a red and IR light unit, 10-20 min/day on the feet, and a Sperti Fiji UV light on my torso. Those seem to be popular on this forum.

Which basics would you say I lack the most, apart from the sunlight?

### Joseph LeBlanc — 2024-03-07T19:56:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-going-to-heal-my-feet.28502/post-332288>

Some things to consider:

Why stop after 15 min of light at rise and set?

For bedtime, why 9pm year round? Sunset times fluctuate throughout the year...

For omega 6, do seasonally available foods in your area fluctuate in omega 6 content throughout the year? If so, should you always keep omega 6 low?

Lastly, why distilled water over spring water?

Well, when does the sun rise, and why would you change your programming with an artificial light source that is different from your environment? Where has anyone here recommended the use of the panels?

You need a lot longer than the 5–15 minutes of sunrise to stimulate everything. Eat at sunrise, get outside, and stay outside for 2 hours or more. If you have to work, crack the door open and make sure you get natural light all day long.

What is your light source after sunset? I was going to sleep after 7 p.m. and would sleep till 7 a.m., usually when i lived high latitude.

What is your mitochondria Haplotype? Could explain your struggles with cold adaption.
[Is Your Mitochondrial Haplotype Coupled Or Uncoupled?](https://www.bluelightdiet.com/blog/mitochondrialhaplotype)

wheat-grass:

You are basically ingesting fake food; everything you eat and digest should be grown under the sun. Jack has a lot of articles about indoor-grown food being loaded with deuterium.

supplements:
Older articles Jack did recommend some supplements, but he doesn't really do it now from what i can see. I personally find them more problematic and cause more issues than benefits. Coconut definitely does not grow in your environment

Sperti use
I believe people use them but a lot of use it is recommend to use them only during the solar noon cycle during the winter time and not inside which is what it sound like your doing as i believe you want more spectrum on your skin at all times for protection.

### NicklasK — 2024-03-08T15:19:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-going-to-heal-my-feet.28502/post-332304>

> [Joseph LeBlanc said:](/goto/post?id=332288)

> Some things to consider:
>
> Why stop after 15 min of light at rise and set?
>

>
> For omega 6, do seasonally available foods in your area fluctuate in omega 6 content throughout the year? If so, should you always keep omega 6 low?
>
> Lastly, why distilled water over spring water?

Yes, I will definitely increase the sun exposure as much as I can. It's already way more now than before I found Jack Kruse. Today, I actually spent more than an hour outside working in the garden with a naked torso, as it was full sun and no wind. The temperature was 7C (45F).

Regarding bedtime, I've read that the body's circadian rhythms like stability. But they also like to follow the seasons, so I think it would also be good to adjust the bedtime accordingly. I will try various ways and see what works best for me.

Regarding omega-6, I think our knowledge at this point indicates sufficiently strongly that there's a very big benefit to not getting anywhere near the amount that the general public is getting. It can also get too low, of course, but I'm eating chicken, almonds and evening primrose oil, so I'm sufficiently certain that it's nowhere near too low for me. It may even still be excessive, but it's definitely way better than the average of the population.

Regarding spring water, I would have to be sufficiently convinced that the water was pure enough that it would make the plastic bottles and cost worth it. Also, I'm adding orthosilicic acid to the water, which seems to be better with distilled water (book: Dennis Crouse: silicon water).

Yes, but it still gave impressive results for nerve regeneration in the study I linked earlier, so I am willing to run the risk.

Yes, there's also mussels and clams, but I don't like those very much, so I prefer to just eat more oysters. Those are higher on Jack's list anyway.

> [pfedorow said:](/goto/post?id=332292)

>
> wheat-grass:
>
> You are basically ingesting fake food; everything you eat and digest should be grown under the sun. Jack has a lot of articles about indoor-grown food being loaded with deuterium.

> Sperti use
> I believe people use them but a lot of use it is recommend to use them only during the solar noon cycle during the winter time and not inside which is what it sound like your doing as i believe you want more spectrum on your skin at all times for protection.

I saw Bryan Johnson use such a full spectrum panel upon waking, and he's certainly doing well. He's also eating a nearly vegan diet though (in a much warmer and brighter climate than mine), so him doing well is not enough for me to conclude anything completely, so I wanted to test it on myself. However, it might not be easy to conclude what the long-term effects on me would be.

Yes, I am already working on getting the amounts of sunlight up significantly.

My light source in the evening is a 2700 K bulb, and I'm using blue blockers during the last few hours before bed. I might change to a 1600 K bulb.

I'm sleeping 8-9 hours per night, so during the winter, there will be some hours where I want some kind of supplementary light. I'm also using a computer, so obviously I will not be able to get to 100% natural sunlight as my only light, and watching the sun the entire day, so my goal is to see if there's a way to get maybe 80% of the potential benefit, and still using some modern appliances some of the time.

I do see it as a realistic goal to eventually get up to at least 2 hours of sunlight a day during the entire year.

I don't know what my mitochondrial hapotype is, what is the best way to find out? A google search didn't result in an obvious answer. My mother is very nordic, 6 ft tall, blonde, so there's a good chance I have a typical scandinavian mitochondrial haplotype, I assume. However, she also has trouble with the cold, so how knows?

Regarding wheatgrass: I assume that Jack bases his opinion on indoor-grown hydroponic foods, grown to full size under artifical light, such as hydroponic tomatoes or lettuce, in which case I agree completely, those are basically fake foods. This does warrant the question as to whether wheatgrass and microgreens would also be in the fake category - however, there's plenty of evidence that indoor-grown wheatgrass is strongly health-promoting, which is a higher level of evidence than the general observation that the hydroponic + artificial light plants have a negative effect on the body. One of the reasons for this is probably that wheatgrass and microgreens are only grown for a very short time before harvest, and during that time, they draw upon the "stored condensed sunlight" that is in the seed.

Can you give a link to some of Jack's articles on indoor grown foods being loaded with deuterium? I'm a member of his Patreon, in case they are gated there.

Even if they are loaded with deuterium, drinking enough 25 ppm DDW water should more than counterbalance it, right? And what amount of deuterium are we talking? Microgreens and wheatgrass do not represent a very large total percentage of a daily total food intake.

### pfedorow — 2024-03-27T17:21:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-going-to-heal-my-feet.28502/post-332880>

> [NicklasK said:](/goto/post?id=332304)
>
> Yes, I will definitely increase the sun exposure as much as I can. It's already way more now than before I found Jack Kruse. Today, I actually spent more than an hour outside working in the garden with a naked torso, as it was full sun and no wind. The temperature was 7C (45F).
>
> Regarding bedtime, I've read that the body's circadian rhythms like stability. But they also like to follow the seasons, so I think it would also be good to adjust the bedtime accordingly. I will try various ways and see what works best for me.
>
> Regarding omega-6, I think our knowledge at this point indicates sufficiently strongly that there's a very big benefit to not getting anywhere near the amount that the general public is getting. It can also get too low, of course, but I'm eating chicken, almonds and evening primrose oil, so I'm sufficiently certain that it's nowhere near too low for me. It may even still be excessive, but it's definitely way better than the average of the population.
>
> Regarding spring water, I would have to be sufficiently convinced that the water was pure enough that it would make the plastic bottles and cost worth it. Also, I'm adding orthosilicic acid to the water, which seems to be better with distilled water (book: Dennis Crouse: silicon water).
>
> Yes, but it still gave impressive results for nerve regeneration in the study I linked earlier, so I am willing to run the risk.
>
> Yes, there's also mussels and clams, but I don't like those very much, so I prefer to just eat more oysters. Those are higher on Jack's list anyway.
>
> I saw Bryan Johnson use such a full spectrum panel upon waking, and he's certainly doing well. He's also eating a nearly vegan diet though (in a much warmer and brighter climate than mine), so him doing well is not enough for me to conclude anything completely, so I wanted to test it on myself. However, it might not be easy to conclude what the long-term effects on me would be.
>
> Yes, I am already working on getting the amounts of sunlight up significantly.
>
> My light source in the evening is a 2700 K bulb, and I'm using blue blockers during the last few hours before bed. I might change to a 1600 K bulb.
>
> I'm sleeping 8-9 hours per night, so during the winter, there will be some hours where I want some kind of supplementary light. I'm also using a computer, so obviously I will not be able to get to 100% natural sunlight as my only light, and watching the sun the entire day, so my goal is to see if there's a way to get maybe 80% of the potential benefit, and still using some modern appliances some of the time.
>
> I do see it as a realistic goal to eventually get up to at least 2 hours of sunlight a day during the entire year.
>
> I don't know what my mitochondrial hapotype is, what is the best way to find out? A google search didn't result in an obvious answer. My mother is very nordic, 6 ft tall, blonde, so there's a good chance I have a typical scandinavian mitochondrial haplotype, I assume. However, she also has trouble with the cold, so how knows?
>
> Regarding wheatgrass: I assume that Jack bases his opinion on indoor-grown hydroponic foods, grown to full size under artifical light, such as hydroponic tomatoes or lettuce, in which case I agree completely, those are basically fake foods. This does warrant the question as to whether wheatgrass and microgreens would also be in the fake category - however, there's plenty of evidence that indoor-grown wheatgrass is strongly health-promoting, which is a higher level of evidence than the general observation that the hydroponic + artificial light plants have a negative effect on the body. One of the reasons for this is probably that wheatgrass and microgreens are only grown for a very short time before harvest, and during that time, they draw upon the "stored condensed sunlight" that is in the seed.
>
> Can you give a link to some of Jack's articles on indoor grown foods being loaded with deuterium? I'm a member of his Patreon, in case they are gated there.
>
> Even if they are loaded with deuterium, drinking enough 25 ppm DDW water should more than counterbalance it, right? And what amount of deuterium are we talking? Microgreens and wheatgrass do not represent a very large total percentage of a daily total food intake.

Bryan Johnson also has an umbrella for sun exposure and is pale as a ghost , define doing good?
Wheatgrass:
I believe if you search deuterium , you will find a lot of info on patreon.

"If its impossible to grow in your environment , you shouldn't eat it. "

[https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-your-maternal-haplogroup.20048/](https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-your-maternal-haplogroup.20048/)

I just got the results back from a hormone spit test, and I'm not able to interpret it fully. It seems to me that having above reference range testosterone, DHEA and progesterone is probably very good, but what about having estrogen slightly above reference as a man - is this good as well? And how much can I conclude from having very high morning cortisol? I'm stopping all electronics at 6 pm, winding down/reading, and then going to bed at 9 pm every night and sleeping well most days.

### NicklasK — 2024-06-20T12:26:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-going-to-heal-my-feet.28502/post-335927>

I also got the results from a blood fatty acid test, which is attached.

My main conclusion is that I will go back to taking 10 mL of fish oil every day, as I had gotten away from that for a while for various reasons. I will also take 1 tsp of evening primrose oil as that seems to be the most convincing source of GLA, and I'd like my blood GLA to be much higher. I am also not in immediate danger of getting too high LA, so that leaves some room for the LA in the primrose oil. Of course, there may be significant differences between optimal values and reference values?

I will also cut down on the omega-6 nuts (almonds, cashews, walnuts).

Edit: Oh, and I'm also aiming at eating 36 oysters per month, as well as canned pacific salmon, sardines, cod liver and cod roe.

Is there anything else that is important to glean from the results?

### NicklasK — 2024-06-21T11:51:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-going-to-heal-my-feet.28502/post-335966>

> [pfedorow said:](/goto/post?id=335949)
>
> Confused why would you take fish oil when your eating it?
>
>
> ### [Dr. Jack Kruse](https://www.facebook.com/drjackkruse/posts/why-i-continually-say-eat-your-damn-seafooddha-from-fish-oil-doesnt-cross-the-bl/2292376074160067/)
>
> Why I continually say, "Eat your damn seafood." DHA from fish oil doesn't cross the blood-brain barrier (BBB) well (if at all). nnEMF opens the BBB pathologically and ruins the Bazan effect. In...
>
>  www.facebook.com

Even with sporadic fish oil intake and eating fish 2-3 times per week, my DHA and EPA were lower than I wanted them to be, so I thought that it would be quite difficult to get the right levels without supplemental oil. But thanks for the link to Jack's facebook post, I had missed/forgotten that DHA from fish oil doesn't get much into the brain.

So I looked at the numbers, and I assume aiming for 2 g/day of combined DHA and EPA from fish and seafood is a good target. Do you agree?

Based on [https://ods.od.nih.gov/factsheets/Omega3FattyAcids-HealthProfessional/](https://ods.od.nih.gov/factsheets/Omega3FattyAcids-HealthProfessional/)
pacific salmon: 187 g for 2g DHA+EPA
sardines: 142 g for 2g DHA+EPA
shrimps: 714 g for 2g DHA+EPA
oysters: 322g for 2g DHA+EPA (56g per edible oyster? -> 5.8 oysters)
mackerel: 166 g for 2g DHA+EPA
cod liver: 21.5g for 2g DHA+EPA

So will it make sense to eat one of these foods every day at the listed amounts? Those are the seafoods that are the most available and palatable to me. I might have a hard time eating 714 g shrimps in a day though. Cod liver is a lot easier!

### NicklasK — 2024-06-22T13:48:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-going-to-heal-my-feet.28502/post-335994>

I studied a bit further, and it seems that sardines, salmon and cod liver all have a high percentage of their DHA in the sn-2 position, which can easily get into the brain.

However, I also saw a comment by Jack in the facebook post, and it seems to conflict with the scientific article #2 below.

"am I wasting my money on my Carlson's Cod Liver Oil?" Jack: "Yep"

[https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2095809920300400](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2095809920300400)

### [Elucidation of triacylglycerols in cod liver oil by liquid chromatography electrospray tandem ion-trap mass spectrometry](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0039914010005011)

Though liquid chromatography electrospray tandem mass spectrometry (LC–ESI-MS2) has been widely used in the structural elucidation of triacylglycerols…

 www.sciencedirect.com

"The positional distribution of FAs in the TAG of cod liver oil obtained by the benchmark lipase method is shown in Table 1. The total FAs composition analysis indicated that cod liver oil is principally characterized by 18:1n-9 (17.56%), 16:0 (11.88%), 20:1n-9 (9.95%), DHA (9.55%) and EPA (8.54%). In addition, the results in Table 1 showed that ω-3 FAs such as DHA (96.04%), 18:4n-3 (78.12%), DPA (75.81%), EPA (75.28%), 20:4n-3 (65.42%) and 16:3n-3 (60.39%) are mainly located at the sn-2"

### Brent Patrick — 2024-06-26T00:41:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-going-to-heal-my-feet.28502/post-336154>

Open a fish oil capsule or Cod liver oil capsule and most smell rancid ,there is a reason they put rosemary or lemon flavour in the Oil to cover the stench.

Break open capsules put both in the freezer in the cap of the bottle and most oils go gelatinous instead of staying low viscosity this is due to different amount of polyunsaturated and saturated fats. If the fish oil is lower in saturated fats and higher in unsaturated fats it stays clear and takes a much colder environment to freeze solid. The other issue is sn2.

Eat seafood it is how nature intended, takes away the many what ifs of production Oils.

### Jims — 2024-06-26T12:27:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-going-to-heal-my-feet.28502/post-336159>

> [Brent Patrick said:](/goto/post?id=336154)
>
> Open a fish oil capsule or Cod liver oil capsule and most smell rancid ,there is a reason they put rosemary or lemon flavour in the Oil to cover the stench.
>
> Break open capsules put both in the freezer in the cap of the bottle and most oils go gelatinous instead of staying low viscosity this is due to different amount of polyunsaturated and saturated fats. If the fish oil is lower in saturated fats and higher in unsaturated fats it stays clear and takes a much colder environment to freeze solid. The other issue is sn2.
>
> Eat seafood it is how nature intended, takes away the many what ifs of production Oils.
>
>

I will do this test and we do eat seafood quite a lot. I have to buy seafood carefully as well as most is not frozen at sea, we don’t live near the coast and my son does have a histamine problem. We see this with frozen wild salmon which we buy of very high quality. Itchy skin and with supermarket salmon even when wild it is worse. But cod, haddock and hake are frozen at sea from the fishmonger we buy online and delivered frozen. Oysters seem to be fine for him bought frozen and then cooked. The brand of cod liver oil we are currently using doesn’t smell rancid nor does it contain rosemary or other extracts. Rosita does smell a bit fishy not rancid and does contain trace amounts of rosemary extract, this is the reason we had stopped using it years ago. The canned cod liver in its own oil would need some sort of production as well I’m assuming? There is no way my 12 year old is going to devour it either. We have to be realistic about this. With his sensitivities I wouldn’t give anything canned for now. I don’t think we can achieve perfection in any food or supplement in the times we live in. I’ve learnt not to give up on things too quickly now especially if they are closest to what best we can find and improvements follow. Cold pressed, Icelandic no additives, third party tested for now this is the aim

I ordered the test from gettested.io, which only serves people who live in the EU.

Fortunately I've been on many of the things on the recommended list for a long time, so methylfolate, choline and SAM-e seem to be those that have been missing the most.

Folate: "Symptoms include fatigue, weakness, mouth sores and neurological issues." This seems like it could be very relevant for me!

SAMe seems to be most used to treat depression, osteoarthritis and liver disease - none of these seem to be a problem for me, but who knows, maybe it'll make something better if I take it.

Choline has the following potential symptoms:
low energy levels/fatigue, memory loss, cognitive decline, learning disabilities, muscle aches, nerve damage, mood changes or disorders

\- fatigue and nerve damage are very relevant for me, so choline might potentially make a significant difference as well!

Consider that it may not be your folate intake that's the problem. It may be a folate receptor/function problem. There is a goldilocks range for folate. Your body will make adjustments to serum folate, folate function and folate receptors based on your environment. There is a very strong link between folate, the biopterin pathway and EMF sensitivity. The eye is involved. You are unlikely to find clear-cut answers in peer-reviewed journals alone, you may have to look peripherally.

This question (until recently) looked normal, ordinary, to me.
**For decades I am taking daily Lugol solution, about 1cc at the time, (one dropperful).**
I assumed that I should have plenty of Iodine.

Imagine my surprise on my recent test
I am low on Potassium Iodide and iodine.

Lugol's solution is (Iodine plus potassium iodide).

Still not sure what to do about that.

### [Cellular Micronutrient Assay (CMA) Test](https://previmedica.com/products/cellular-micronutrient-assay-cma-test)

The Cellular Micronutrient Assay (CMA) tests for insufficiencies in micronutrients (vitamins & minerals), amino acids, & other nutrients within immune cells.

 previmedica.com

[https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jansz-journal.28468/post-335523](https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jansz-journal.28468/post-335523)

### NicklasK — 2024-07-22T09:37:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-going-to-heal-my-feet.28502/post-337469>

I've been working on upping my protein intake, as it seems to have been way too low for many years. I found a conservative advice for protein intake - 1 g protein per kg bodyweight. It seems much of the research concludes that 1.6 g/kg is more optimal, but even 1 g/kg is challenging for me. Furthermore, since I'm not moving around that much, I think I have a lower need anyway. I am doing weightlifting 2 times a week as my main exercise type.

I'm trying to figure out how much of each of the types of protein would be approximately ideal for me.

The main protein sources I have access to that work well for me:

oysters
shrimp
canned pacific wild-caught salmon
canned sardines
canned cod liver
grass-fed beef liver
grass-fed ground beef
organic skinless chicken

I know that Jack prioritizes oysters first, seafood second, fish third etc., however oysters every day would be too expensive, and it doesn't take that much fish/seafood for me to reach 2 g DHA+EPA per day, so I'm wondering what I should ideally fill the rest of my protein demand with?

I'm aiming at eating 60 g of animal protein a day (my weight is 75 kg and I'm getting about 15 g/day protein from other sources), so that would be 420 g/week.

If I eat the following in a week, I'll get about 2 g DHA+EPA per day:

90 g canned cod liver: 8 g DHA+EPA
200 g canned salmon: 2 g DHA+EPA
200 g sardines: 3 g DHA+EPA
700 g shrimp: 2 g DHA+EPA

It'll also get me 240 g protein out of the 420 g weekly demand, leaving 180 g missing. My question is, should I fill out the remaining 180 g also with seafood and fish, or will it be just as good at this point to fill it out with beef liver, beef and skinless chicken?

### Woodstock — 2024-10-15T17:12:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-going-to-heal-my-feet.28502/post-341052>

> [JanSz said:](/goto/post?id=330670)
>
> [@NicklasK](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/30245/)
>
> Are you (your feet) possibly affected by some situations listed below?
> Reading the below, I was especially thinking about some dental work that could have affected positions of your cranial bones.
>
> Remember, my chiropractor, in regard to your feet problem, he thought of adjusting C1, C2 and moving your cranial bones.
> \=================
> Have you checked a pressure in your eyeballs?
>
> This pressure check is called tonometry. In the past, you may have had an eye pressure test using a puff of air. Now most ophthalmologists use a more accurate device that measures pressure by direct contact with the eye.
>
> Normal eye pressure ranges from 12-22 mm Hg.
> Find out this number. Do that yearly.
> If gets too close to 22 it is time to act.
>
> \=================
> Your body is naturally asymmetrical.
> Balance is maintained through the integration of these system imbalances.
> When balanced integration fails, structural weaknesses and pain can develop.
> \---
> While walking barefoot outdoors on uneven terrain is a great way to ground, walking barefoot on hard, flat surfaces, such as tile or concrete, can cause problems, including bunions.
> \---
> Things like eyeglasses, LASIK surgery, dental implants and root canals can affect your sensory experience, and hence your gait and posture, which in turn can result in pain syndromes.
> \---
> Dental work can affect your bite, which affects your cranial and cervical posture.
> If teeth are pulled and not replaced with an implant, your brain may not be able to determine where your center of gravity is, causing your body to overcompensate to one side or the other.
> \----
> LASIK eye surgery can trigger unexpected problems.
> One of the main problems is that if it fixes you into a set prescription that is wrong, it can lock in dysfunction.
> With glasses, you’re not locked into one prescription.
> You can change it and get new glasses if it turns out that your symmetry was off.
>
> /////////

Dental implants cause problems and not getting an implant causes problems. I guess I’m fucked???

I love to munch on butter beans (I am lazy and buy organic canned ones) in between meals or sometimes in the night if I cant sleep, or when I am driving and feeling stressed. I slowly munch on one bean at the time imagining the tasty soft butter beans being small sponges, assimilating stress hormones(and other toxins) from my gut/bile. It makes me so relaxed. Or I make me a smoothie with hot water, 1/2 cup of butter beans, fresh garlic, and sea salt.. and drink it like a hot broth in between meals to have best effect.

In addition to psyllium, all sort of beans (except soy), like lentils, peas, chickpeas, butter beans, have tons of soluble fiber. To reach therapeutic effect one needs about 3 cups a day of beans or several spoonful of psyllium.

I looked around a bit, and I was unable to find evidence that soluble fiber binds to fats to a meaningful degree - this would be a very significant thing, as it would then carry the fat out of the body, and prevent us from making use of it.

Soluble fiber does slow down the absorption of fat, as well as potentially trapping some fat within the fiber gel.

Do you have any sources on the binding to fats effect? I would be interested in reading about it.

it is super hard to find anything about it really. Not much research done. Pubmed has some research on Psyllium/Cholesterol that might give some clues

### [Nutriceuticals in cardiovascular disease: psyllium - PubMed](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17438377/)

In recent years, there has been a growing interest in the use of dietary fiber in health maintenance and disease prevention. A deficiency of fiber in the Western diet may be contributing to the current epidemics of diabetes mellitus, coronary artery disease (CAD), and colonic cancer. The...

 pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov

### [Dietary fiber for reducing blood cholesterol - PubMed](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/2169487/)

Soluble dietary fiber, including psyllium, has been shown to have mild to modest cholesterol lowering properties. Psyllium hydrophilic mucilloid (Metamucil, Procter and Gamble, Cincinnati, OH), a bulk laxative, when used as a dietary supplement in combination with a low fat diet, can lower total...

 pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov

"Soluble dietary fiber, including psyllium, has been shown to have mild to modest cholesterol lowering properties. Psyllium hydrophilic mucilloid (Metamucil, Procter and Gamble, Cincinnati, OH), a bulk laxative, when used as a dietary supplement **in combination with a low fat diet,** can lower total serum cholesterol and low-density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol, and raise high-density lipoprotein (HDL) cholesterol in hypercholesterolemic patients. The cholesterol lowering effects of psyllium are much less evident than those observed with cholestyramine and the HMG-CoA reductase inhibitors. **The mechanism for the cholesterol lowering effect of psyllium is not known**. The substance is well tolerated with long-term use."

"In recent years, there has been a growing interest in the use of dietary fiber in health maintenance and disease prevention. A deficiency of fiber in the Western diet may be contributing to the current epidemics of diabetes mellitus, coronary artery disease (CAD), and colonic cancer. The awareness of fiber as a dietary supplement may have contributed to the reported 30% decline in death rate from CAD observed over the past 15 years. **Psyllium is a soluble gel-forming fiber that has been shown to bind to the bile acids in the gut and prevent their normal reabsorption, similar to the bile acid sequestrant drugs.** Psyllium is useful as an adjunct to dietary therapy (step 1 or step 2 American Heart Association \[AHA\] diet) in the treatment of patients with mild-to-moderate hypercholesterolemia. In combination with other cholesterol-lowering drugs, such as statins, psyllium provides an added benefit on cholesterol lowering, and is well tolerated and cost-effective."

I bolded the places that might give a clue.
So the fact that the soluble fiber therapy works well only when eating the fiber separate from fat, kinda is proof that the fat is assimilated from the soluble fiber too. Bile is kinda of a fat too.

I checked my cholesterol levels after 1,5 years of eating legumes every day and it had dropped 30% from original. I always had sky high cholesterol. Nothing dropped it. My other labs were all perfect too. I didnt do this to drop my cholesterol, because I was never worried about it. I was trying to heal my gallbladder and my burnout. It has helped those a lot too. So I can say it is a success so far. My stress hormone level is way lower now, I can tell. But yeah, I stopped alcohol and coffee consumption too (just having them very occasionally now)

"The mechanism for the cholesterol lowering effect of psyllium is not known"

It seems that the interaction between soluble fiber and bile salts is caused by hydrophobic interactions, hydrogen bonds and ionic interaction, and that the bond between bile salts and soluble fiber is much stronger than the bond between fats in general and soluble fiber.

I also saw references to the effect of cholesterol reduction also happening in high-fat diets, but those articles were paywalled.

The structure and bonding character of cholesterol is very different from that of regular saturated fats, and a significant difference in bonding ability by the bulk of the molecule would be expected.

Regular fats have an acid part that can bond in various ways, which cholesterol does not have. Instead, it has an alcohol group which is very different in its bonding capabilities.

Another explanation for why the article you reference specifies "low-fat" is that if you're eating high-fat (particularly animal fat), that raises the cholesterol level, and there will be less likelihood of a net drop in cholesterol, even if assuming that soluble fiber doesn't bind regular fat at all.

For these reasons, I still believe that soluble fiber bonding to regular fats will be negligible compared to their binding to bile salts.

I hope that the psyllium will drop my cortisol as well, as that was also too high. Could be great if it reduces both my cortisol and my estrogen, while not reducing testosterone and progesterone!

Have you done other things that helped significantly with your burnout? My energy has gotten better, but it still needs work.

Interesting. You can do a hack and see if anything changes if you continue eating Psyllium with fat!
I have done it without because I was desperate for change. I have tried to follow the soluble fiber expert Karen Hurds advice as good as I could because I do know she has helped so many people heal, she has very high success rate when people stick to it (which many dont because its tough)
So for my burnout, I chose to immediately stop working that much. I almost cut my work in half. Resting more. Working on Trauma has been a big thing, but it has been so well accompanied with the bean/lentil eating as they have a calming effect on me.
I went for a trip to Central America and the US last fall and camping on the beach was really really healing experience. But yeah most part of my healing happened here in Finland at home doing nature in summer and in winter resting a lot. I have the opportunity to go camping in Mexico again in a month so I am going to do that, to take care of the little rest of burnout that is still present.

I stopped doing CT or freezing, keeping myself comfortable warm instead per Karens advice. She says adrenal fatigue needs rest and relaxation, no stimulants also no cold - not even sauna because too hot is also a stressor. I did do sauna still though, because we love it in Finland - but I did it less and not that hot.

Eating adequate amount of animal protein at least 3 times a day has also been great. No fasting. I did gain weight the first year, but I didnt care. I knew its part of the healing process. I have lost 10 kg already from what I gained, 5 kg to go and I am back where I used to be. And the weight has dropped without hunger or fasting.

I have to say there is a big trauma component to my burnout thing I am very certain. That is why I do trauma work on myself. It is all about inner relaxation. But I have to have the capacity to be silent, and meet the unwanted feelings that emerge, and that capacity is really increasing through this program. I used to take a glass of wine, or nicotine gum, or coffee, or anything else to soothe myself. I dont need that anymore.

One of the big things that has helped for me has also been finding ways to avoid being in sympathetic activation too much of the time.

From what I've found, it's way better to be in significant stress much of the time and then be in complete rest a small part of the time regularly, than it is to just be slightly stressed all the time.

And if you're burned out, you of course have to be in complete rest more of the time, until you catch your footing again.

For a long time, I could feel that sitting close to a computer monitor had a significant negative effect on my energy, but getting a 65" tv as a computer monitor that sits 3m away from me doesn't cause that problem.

I would also really like to go somewhere where I can be on the beach and swim in the ocean year-round, but that would be very difficult for me in my current situation, as I'm not walking. Fortunately, I have experienced more progress with the healing of my feet in the last few months than in a long time. If that turns out not to be enough, I might grit my teeth and go to El Salvator for some months!

Sounds really good with your progress! I have a lot of respect for it when people do effective work on trauma so that they don't need compensatory behavior such as addictions any longer. EFT (emotional freedom technique) and Joe Dispenza guided meditations have been very effective for me in this area, and also Donna Eden energy medicine.

Yes, the sympathetic/parasympathetic balance is definitely a critical part of healing.

One of the main new things I've been doing is to put an 80% DMSO solution on my feet and all the way up the outsie of my legs to my hip, letting it sit for 5 mins, then rubbing castor oil on top of it and then blowing on it with a Prife iTeraCare device which blows hot air and also terahertz radiation associated with healthy cells on it. You might get the same results just using a hair dryer or other heat source, I'm not sure the terahertz part does anything.

But I've been able to start training my feet and legs a lot more, and not running into re-injuring myself which is something I've done many times.

One thing which has taken me a bunch of years to figure out is that I believe that I have diverticulitis or something similar (small "sacs" protruding from the large intestine which can push on other tissues), which might be responsible for my lower back problems and pushing on my spine at L4-L5, which is the area that the nerves going to the feet spring from. This is one of the reasons I became focused on eating more fiber, as a diet low in fiber can contribute to this problem.

### NicklasK — 2026-03-31T11:27:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-going-to-heal-my-feet.28502/post-370022>

Please help me stop my mom's cognitive decline!

Recently, my 75-year-old mom has exhibited some worrying symptoms. Occasionally during the last 3 years, we have done online chess puzzles together, and while I was significantly faster and better at it than her, she did very well relative to her age group.

A few days ago, I asked her to play chess against the weakest AI, and it took her a very long time to do some very basic moves. She's able to do most basic day-to-day things, but doing online banking has become more difficult for her, and handwriting is also harder.

She's underweight, but has decent energy levels and is very athletic for her age, though not very physically active.

She is also complaining that something feels off about her right arm - like it's a little bit separate from the rest of her body.

I'm very alarmed by all of this, and she is too. Fortunately, she's now willing to do a lot of the things I want her to do to get better, where previously it has been difficult to get her to make changes.

Her diet is pretty good - eggs, bacon, butter, vegetables (e.g. celery, cucumber, arugula) for breakfast, and grass-fed beef with vegetables and butter in the evening, with some variation. Also occasionally sardines, herring. She eats purple corn some days of the week as a starch source - the purple pigment strongly reduces the potential harm of the starch. The corn is prepared traditionally by nixtamalization (boiling with calcium hydroxide, which neutralizes the phytic acid). She occasionally eats beef liver.

My current sense is that something is bothering her brain, whether it be aluminum (check out Dennis Crouse's books, he talks about especially orthosilicic acid in water to get rid of aluminum, as well as using selenium and other nutrients to help the brain. He significantly reversed Alzheimer's in his own mom with his procedure).

It's also interesting that this problem happened after a long winter in Denmark is just finishing.

I have her on the following program, please help me figure out if I'm missing anything important, or if something should be modified/removed:

\- sunlight 5+ minutes in the morning with the bare feet on wet soil. I try to get her to be outside as much as possible, which is easy in summer but hard the rest of the year

\- orthosilicic acid in drinking water (removes aluminum from the body)

\- getting a shower filter soon

\- infrared sauna 3-4x/week

\- 6 g creatine per day

\- jump rope several times per week

\- HIIT, or more accurately, moderate intensity interval running, every 4th day

\- skin brushing

\- 3 minutes of Sperti Fiji UV light every 3rd day

\- Donna Eden energy medicine exercises ([https://edenenergymedicine.com/alzheimers-disease/](https://edenenergymedicine.com/alzheimers-disease/))

\- broccoli sprouts (sulfopraphane, this has shown promising results in reducing the symptoms in autistic kids)

\- Dr. Richard Schulze's program for alzheimer's/dementia (his book "Save your life" seems to have been almost scrubbed from existence probably by the powers that shouldn't be, but I found it and you can download it here: [https://www.dropbox.com/s/386jybyqf7v8o13/Schulze-docs.zip?dl=0](https://www.dropbox.com/s/386jybyqf7v8o13/Schulze-docs.zip?dl=0)). This includes intense cleansing of kidney, liver, intestines, blood, with a lot of different herbs. He seems to have impressive results with bringing back the minds of old people whose mind had gone completely

\- deadlift with a 24 kg kettlebell

\- regarding her thyroid (which is supposed to also be implicated in cognitive impairment), she has been thin for many years, constant cold hands and feet, thinning hair, and issues with nails. She hasn't had her thyroid tested. In my country, Denmark, the doctors mainly just want to test TSH, and if that is off, test T4, and if that is low, give synthetic T4. I'm highly skeptical about that, and there are many people in Denmark with thyroid issues who are not helped by the system. I've heard Jack talk about how UV light is critical for the thyroid, so I'm hoping the Sperti will help there.

It's difficult to get her to go outside with bare skin if it's even slightly cold, so it's hard to get much natural UV for her here. I know, many of you will say that we should go to El Salvador, and I'd like to do that, but I'm not able to walk, so that's quite difficult. And she won't go there alone.

What's the best way to increase natural UV exposure for someone who tends to be very cold and therefore has an aversion to be outside without clothes on except during summer?

So my current plan for her thyroid is for her to consistently use the Sperti Fiji, and I'll give her some dessicated thyroid that I'm getting off iherb, and see if it makes her better. Maybe that'll fix her internal furnace so she'll be willing to be much more outside.

\- there is a wet spot on the ceiling in her bedroom, because a hole in the roof appeared. After looking into water damage and mold, I'm worried that mold toxins could also be a significant factor. So we're going to find someone to fix the roof, and then remediate the mold

She also has vitiligo, which appeared last year. I've told her to use the Sperti Fiji for that, but she was resistant to the idea. Now, as I saidm fortunately she's more cooperative.

I also have a red light panel that's 1 m tall, should I get her to use that as a high priority?

### LoriLee — 2026-03-31T16:37:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-going-to-heal-my-feet.28502/post-370030>

In everything you just listed, I read 5 min of sun each day. Would your tree live with only 5 min of sun per day? Barely. Eventually it would die. Your mom is old so is by default less water so by default needs more sun not less. She's shriveling up from dehydration and not the kind of water you drink. Hire someone to take you both to El Salvador. Stay in costa del sol where it's flat. That's my low bandwidth 2 cents. Sit her by an open window at least. I understand resistant parents. You may just need to be thankful for what youve had til now and worry more about your own problems.

We have some water damage, her symptoms fit 9 out of 13 of the typical ones (CIRS), and I'm starting to have some dizziness when I'm in some parts of the house as well. So we're currently focusing on removing ourselves from the exposure and getting the house remediated asap.

I did get her up to 2x15 minutes of sun exposure, morning and evening, so that's a bit better at least.

> Any advice/experience to share?

> Thank you

There are things you could look at whilst researching slightly longer term goals, possibly tacking on holidays to lower latitudes to check them out for a potential move. My n=1 back up plan is Fuerteventura, where pre covid I would go for some winter sun, because its not a transatlantic flight, so if I moved there its not unrealistic for getting family support from the UK, or getting back to the UK, and my brother lives in mainland Spain. My children are adults, so I don't have the added stress of navigating a new environment with dependent children.

Any lower latitude choice is n=1, there could be multiple "safe" choices in terms of safety from crime, but you have to factor in things like population density, availability of healthcare and the cost, particularly with an existing diagnosis, your children's education etc, and you mention "I" will be moving with my children - so its how realistic getting family or other support would be if you needed it.

Jack posted this yesterday in the Breast Cancer thread in the female quantum biology forum. [https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breast-ca-thread.24086/page-2#post-330200](https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breast-ca-thread.24086/page-2#post-330200)

_"Cancer is a sign that your body is not able to eliminate bad cells before they mutate, replicate, and develop into tumor growths due to epigenetic changes that create problems in the nuclear genome.

We are told, by centralized Mis, that light from the sun causes cancer, but what if I told you it isn't light from the sun but artificial light from our phones, street lights, and man-made electronics instead?

Cancer rates are skyrocketing, especially in females under 30.

For a generation that spends 90% of its time indoors, don't you think it's a little weird that cancer prevalence is up 520% and tied to low vitamin D status? It is a hugely profitable machine for hospitals. Centralized science is so quick to blame the sun with little to no mention of:

• Non-native electromagnetic radiation
• Environmental triggers
• Chemical load
• Inflammation
• Artificial light exposure
• Emotional trauma/stress
\*junk foods/ processed foods circadian mismatched foods for zip code

Artificial light at night suppresses melanin, melatonin, while raising cortisol. Traditionally speaking, our body would sense the light in our environment along with the absence of blue light after sunset. Our body would then read this signal and start secreting melatonin from our colony of mitochondria.

In order to optimize your body's natural cell cleanup cycles, you need to optimize your light environment to give your body the melanin and melatonin it needs to govern these cycles. If you have cancer, you need to be optimizing your sleep quality and repair cycles which means blocking artificial light at night as a minimum. You get diagnosed with chronic illness where you end up inside a hospital. While inside the hospital, you receive free medical treatment which includes:

Nutrition that is: • Not local or seasonal (circadian mismatch) • Heavy in deuterium (supports cancer growth) • Heavy in artificial color and preservatives (damages cells) • High sugar content (promotes cancer growth) • Low in animal protein and fat • Water that is filled with chemicals and pesticides."_

As well as the light, water and magnetism angles, it might be an idea to do an audit of those factors Jack has listed, based on where you are now, which could help you assess potential good places to move to.

Panama seems to be safe. I see kids on the roadside and nobody bothers them.
Overall in Panama in the countryside I've felt exceptionally safe
No need to worry. Everyone says Buenas which is a truncated form of Buenas Dias/ tardes. Not had any problems whatsoever.

Plus countryside pop density is just 60 per km average.

### healthy2015 — 2024-01-14T22:13:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/canary-islands-thread.28505/post-330223>

We live in Fuerteventura, one of the Canary Islands, but we are now wondering if it is the best option for us to live there.

It has a low population density (approx 120k).
Latitude 28.3968.
1 underwater volcano approx. 209km.
1 active volcano approx. 375km.
**However…**.
No rain or very little rainfall approx. 101mm per year.
Cannot freely grow plants, veg, etc.
We have Calima’s regularly, where sand and dust is blown over from the Shara desert, which causes unhealthy air quality.
Air quality is generally not good.

Would Tenerife be better?

High population density.
Latitude: 28.2916.
1 underwater volcano approx. 25.47km.
1 active volcano approx. 133km.
More rainfall approx. 1000mm per year.
Lush, and you can grow plants, etc.
Air quality good.

My main concern is the air quality being continuously unhealthy and poor as it is similar to a desert here.

Thanks

### Anthony Smith — 2024-01-14T22:50:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/canary-islands-thread.28505/post-330225>

I think your question gives you your answer and since health is a main concern why not much further south? It is very easy to grow things in the tropics.
A side note, I’ve heard a volcanic eruption can ruin your day . . . I live 50 miles from one

### Garry Kirk — 2024-06-14T19:58:14+0300

I think most fruit and veg is grown in Gran Canaria. When you shop in the Canaries and look to buy local, that seems to be where most local grown food comes from.
Have you been to GC? There are a lot of mountains which catch the moisture and any rain, Tenerife is similar north of Teide. You can drive down from the Observatory or top of the Volcano on that north road and you would be forgiven for thinking you were in the UK.
The north of Tenerife also catches a lot more rain. Personally I much prefer the North of the island.

FV is perfect for being naked. The SE of island is something like 40k of unspoilt beaches and you can take your clothes off without any of the issues you can be faced with elsewhere in Europe, as I'm sure you know. that's what I like about FV.

Maybe the air quality would be better higher up in GC or Tenerife?

G.

### Garry Kirk — 2024-07-13T15:37:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/canary-islands-thread.28505/post-337050>

Anywhere around about Costa Calma and south of there. You can head west to La Pared for sunsets as well, it’s about a 10 minute drive, so you’ll get sunrise and sunset, plus about 40k of naturist beaches.

### eve001 — 2024-08-17T16:49:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/canary-islands-thread.28505/post-338548>

Hola! How are the Canary Islands RF EMFs wise?  Anyone knows of good accommodation? I became EHS in a 5G town and joined here recently. Looking for healing places in/near EU (for now).

### Garry Kirk — 2024-08-20T14:53:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/canary-islands-thread.28505/post-338680>

> [eve001 said:](/goto/post?id=338548)
>
> Hola! How are the Canary Islands RF EMFs wise?  Anyone knows of good accommodation? I became EHS in a 5G town and joined here recently. Looking for healing places in/near EU (for now).

You will get 4g in most built up places around the coasts, most airbnb and hotels have wifi etc. I haven't seen any 5g anywhere but I'm sure it will be on the way to the bigger cities in Tenerife and Gran Canaria if it isn't already there. Best shout is something detached, where you can switch the router off and switch off the fuses, RCBO, RCD's etc, there are even tents in the mountains but you find half the time there is wifi provided in these places as well. Airbnb, booking.com and find your own place away from the herd!

### Pebbles — 2024-09-03T11:08:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/canary-islands-thread.28505/post-339191>

> [eve001 said:](/goto/post?id=338548)
>
> Hola! How are the Canary Islands RF EMFs wise?  Anyone knows of good accommodation? I became EHS in a 5G town and joined here recently. Looking for healing places in/near EU (for now).

As a general rule, you can find secluded areas w very low nnEMF on each island, farther from the beach- cheaper but you need a car permanenetly. Beach - look for nudist or wild beaches, not tourist crowding. The prices are on a large scale, so find what better suits you. Fuerteventura between Costa Calma and Jandia - some secluded apartments near the beach as well.

### Mihaly — 2024-09-11T21:59:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/canary-islands-thread.28505/post-339489>

Hey, is anyone up for a meeting in Fuerteventura?
Im living there long time, mainly in the south but now I try it in the north. I cant compare with other places around the world without experience living elsewhere but I still like it after 7 years. Perfect for a simple, nature friendly lifestyle!
The beaches are amazing, especially in the south. Good quality local fuits and veggies you can find on the market in La Lajita on sundays. Or they say there is also a market in La Oliva in the north.

The strong wind and the calima are not so fun. However I have zero issues with the calima (high redox and good detox?), I can still do my outdoor sports without problem while many 'non mitochondriac' people complain a lot for breathing issues and weakness in those days.
About the EMF strength I dont have any measurment, only my intuitive experiences. As long as I can go in the ocean I feel super, compared to living in a big city before.

More to west its probably less significant dust and more opportunities for everything but Tenerife or even GC is too crowded in my opinion. I love La Palma with the green forests, its more rainy and there are also more fruits&veggies but beachwise I dont find it so good. And a lot of driving up&down on the hills... La Gomera, El Hierro similar.

At the end I dont know if there is a perfect island, so it's a matter of consideration which features are more important.

### Jims — 2024-01-15T16:12:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/burping-constantly.28506/post-330252>

We’re experiencing something strange with my 11yr old son with adhd/ocd, since coming across all the info here we began increasing sea food intake and through the summer everything was going pretty much how it had been earlier. He’s been improving but not quietly. With that I mean lots of strange symptoms come up. We get lots of laughter at times, then suddenly moaning, flares in OCD especially when catching a cold things get worse. And nowadays he’s constantly burping..
Along with increase in sea food like salmon roe 2tsps everyday, cooked Oyesters we also introduced clams and ghee, sometime after this his burping episodes began.
He’s had some small episodes when he was younger as well but usually they would go away in a few days. This has been the longest Ann’s throughout the day. when we first went keto carnivore, fats from meats would cause it too. We were able to stop all enzyme supplements when we reduced meat fats and didn’t get any burping despite eating a lot of meat during this summer. could it be that fat reven from sea food can also cause the same reaction? the salmon roe ? Also to add his speech and cognition and weight is improving despite this so I’m stumped

### Jack Kruse — 2024-01-15T16:43:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/burping-constantly.28506/post-330254>

Lower esophageal issue = vagus nerve issue = redox issue.

Read the water muse blog or the Vagus Rx.
