> [rtd said:](/goto/post?id=340069)
>
> I now use the product NuCalm, non-linear binaural beats with optional usage of their bio-discs. I use their "Rescue, Deep Sleep, and Flow State". Game changer for me. I recognize this product most likely will not be embraced by this forum. To each their own.

rtd, I looked at the NuCalm on their website and it looks like it is simply a binaural beat that supposedly entrains your brain into different brainwave states (research shows no difference between binaural beats and white noise - [https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/22496862/](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/22496862/) ) and an eye mask to put your eyes in complete darkness (Of course, your skin is still exposed to light or you wouldn't need a mask). We all know what the body wants to do in darkness (i.e., sleep) Also, peer reviewed research shows that listening to binaural beats impairs cognitive function - [https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10329717/](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10329717/)
And, the Bio-Discs seem like complete bullshit. Nothing wrong with a placebo effect, but it is probably cheaper to go outside and listen to nature.

### Sandra2nd1 — 2024-11-22T07:06:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-journal-chronic-fatigue-me-and-my-twinbrother.28522/post-342767>

> [Thijs Oosting said:](/goto/post?id=330417)
>
> **Our story:**
>
> Hello everyone. Me and my identical twin brother are from the Netherands. We are 35 years old and would like to share our story and see if anyone can help us out. We have been dealing with severe chronic fatigue for about 7 years now. It came on very suddenly on the same day in our lives and has not left us ever since.
>
> From 6 months old we have had asthma and allergies for animals, perfume, smoke and dust. We started having constant stuffed nose and had to bring multiple handkerchiefs to school. We had lactose intolerance from age 5. At age 9 or so we started noticing more and more digestion issues. Frequent bloating, gassing, burping and stomach pain. At age 20 we stopped eating gluten.
>
> At age 16 we started using Seretide Diskus for our asthma. Even though asthma attacks no longer happened we continued taking Seretide Diskus (which is a corticosteroid). We also started using Beclometason Nevel Spray for our constant all day long sneezing attacks and runny nose. Beclometason Nevel Spray is also a corticosteroid. The spray helped immensily and now we only had to endure 1 day per month of the all day long extreme nose allergy attacks (which almost made us unable to work or go to school).
>
> It seemed all was ok as long we avoided animals and took our medicines. We still had cold hands and feet, dry skin and some other minor issues but life was certainly fun. We worked out a lot and build a lot of muscle.
>
> Even though we were never overweight, we never seemed satiated. We wanted to gain muscle mass and started bodybuilding and eating a lot of calories. We had to eat a ton to try to gain weight. Eating that much had a lot of pressure on the gut and we experienced a lot of gut issues. Therefore, at the age of 28, we decided to go on a dry-fast in Austria to give our gut some rest from food. This was in August 2016. The fast was led by Ray Maor and 30 other people joined us.
>
> Before the fast we had to eat only fruit for 1 week. This was hard for us because we immediately lost a lot of weight, even though we ate more fruit than any of the other members. Then after this week of fruit the fast started.
>
>
> Day 1: Diluted juice (80% juice and 20 % water)
> Day 2: Diluted juice (60% juice)
> Day 3: Diluted juice 30% juice)
> Day 4: only water
> Day 5
> Day 6
> Day 7:
> Day 8:
> Day 9: at noon we started with water
> Day 10: Diluted Juice 30%
> Day 11: Diluted juice 60%
> Day 12: Diluted juice 80%
> Day 13: Juice
>
> After 3 days of dry fasting (meaning no food and water) our fatigue came on like a bom. It probably started during the night. We both woke up in the morning with sever fatigue. It was like a switch had been pulled. None of the other participants had any issues.
>
> After the fast we were advised to keep drinking juices for 2 moths. This was extremely hard for us but we did so nevertheless as we thought the fatigue was some kind of detox. After 7 weeks we started eating again but only a slight increase in energy was the result. In the last 7 years we have mostly eaten a grass fed paleo diet and have gone total ketogenic carnivore for a couple of times (each time 7 months or so).
>
> Ever since the dry fast, our fatigue has never left.
>
>
>
> **Lyme disease:**
> In 2022, lyme and co-infections were detected in both of us through a FISH test (blood test with fluorescent light).
>
> In 2023 lyme infection and co-infections were detected via bioresonance. Then later another practioner also deteced lyme and co-infections via bioresonance.
>
> I do believe we have lyme disease. However, it seems unlikely to me that the lyme infection is the only reason why we are fatigued. It seems more likely to me that the adrenal glands were already weakened due to lyme infections and that the dry-fact caused our adrenal glands to collapse. So I am guessing that the reason of our fatigue is a combination of a strong lyme infection, a lot of methylation issues and weak adrenals. However, this is just my interpretation. What do you guys think?
>
>
>
> **How to heal?**
> We have been told by Your Health Concept (a practioner who has studied Jack’s work) to go tot he Yucatan for 3 months. This woulld be enough to heal our fatigue. Lots of grounding, being outside all day, avoiding blue light and nnEMF and eating a carnivore diet.
>
> We find it hard to believe this would be enough, since fort he last 2 summers we did not feel any different when sunning a lot. Sure we did not follow the protocol, but summer or winter never made any difference in our fatigue. 2 years ago we had a Vitamin D level of 5 ng/ml. We build it up to 56 ng/ml (October 2023). This is still too low but we do not feel any difference at all.
>
> We are currently in the yucatan (for 3 weeks already) and we will continue for 3 months. Grounding all day, staying outside and following the rest of the protocol. We do not feel any better at all and we wonder if we need extra adrenal support (Cycloset/hydrocortisone) or something else.
>
> Any advice on how to heal would be welcome. See lab results below. All tests have been done in the last 7 years while we were fatigued. Levels of fatigue have only fluctuated slightly depending on type of diet we followed the last 7 years. The higher in carbs the better we feel in general.
>
>
> .............................................................................................................................................................
>
> **Remarks:**
>
> About 6 month after the our fatigue started, Thijs had a BUN / Creatinine ratio of 8/1. Therefore, I presume, dehydration can not be the most significant factor of our fatigue? It does seem our dehydration has gone progressilvely worse over the last 7 years since our current BUN/Creatinine is 18/1. This makes sense because the fatigue made us mostly couch bound which means we stayed mostly indoors and watched tv (laptop/phone use) for the last 7 years. But I am unsure wether sudden dehydration due to the dry-fast could have led to the sudden significant shift in energy?
>
> \- We both have somewhat Low blood pressure
>
> \- We both feel we need lots of salt (15 gr per day). If we go lower than 15 gr of celtic salt per day we feel even more weak/fatigued. Low Aldosterone?
>
> \-We have tried several times to go on a ketogenic diet. This always goes sort of ok fort he first week but then we start crashing. Could this be because ketosis with weak adrenals sheds to much minerals? I am not sure if it is just minerals because carbohydrates always seem to make us a little bit stronger. Even after being in ketosis for 7 months, when we switch to carbs we feel a bit stronger.
>
> \- Right now we have followed the Leptin protocol for 10 weeks but we do not feel any different at all. It does not feel we are LS. We tried going into ketosis and once again energy started crashing after about a week. Therefore we upped carbs again up to 90 gr per day.
>
> \- We have a double SNP on Leptin. Perhaps we need more than the standard leptin protocol? Or is the lyme messing up the Leptin sensitivity due to inflammation?
>
> \- Potassium is often on the low side in the blood and we have from time to time tried to eat up to 5000 mg of potassium (lots of potatoes). It does seem to help slightly in energy but eating that much potatoes or other potasium rich foods is a challange. Avocado’s give us intense stomach pain.
>
>
>
> **Things that give us some (temporary) energy:**
> We never drink coffee. Also not before our fatigue came on 7 years ago. However we have tried 7 or 8 times to drink coffee since our fatigue. The first time we drink coffee we get a major energy boost and it almost seems our energy problems are gone. We feel happy, positive, legs feel less sour and we can suddenly walk for 2 hours or so. Then after about 2 to 4 hours we crash tot he same fatigue level. If we take another coffee the next day the same happens only to a lesser extend and we start shaking after about 4 hours. Then if we have another coffee the next day we feel unstablily energized, much less energy than the first time and after about 4 hours we experience hypoglycemia. Then we stopped trying the coffee until a few years later. The first or second coffee gave us extreme hypoglycemic attach after about 4 hours. Taking direct sugar does not seem to help very fast and it takes a while to get blood sugar up.
>
> This also happens with green tea, cocao, ginseng and even magic mushrooms. We tried a magic mushroom once and after about 4 hours we got extreme hypoglycemic and we had to get to a hospital at 02:00 AM. One of the worst hypoglycemic events we have had in our life. Ginseng seems to give us some energy but it does not feel stable (irritating energy that makes us tremble). We stopped ginseng because it gave us extreme insomnia.
>
>
>
> **Eating major cheat meals:**
> During the last 7 years of chronic fatigue we have often tried cheat/binge nights on Saturday night. We often ate a litlre of ice cream each of us and a couple of bags of cookies. Presumably eating about 5000 calories each of us in a couple of hours. The weird thing is. We feel much better then. Our energy and mood improves significantly and we can suddenly go for a long walk. 1 time we even were able to jog for about 5 min. Something absolutely unimaginable on normal days. This energy extends for about 1,5 days (into Sunday night or Monday morning). The weird thing is, if we continue to have cheat meals night mutliple times per week the effect wears off and it does not work anymore.
>
>
> **Immune system swith from TH1 to TH2 (or the other way around):**
> Before the dry-fast 7 years ago, when we experienced no fatigue, we still had sinus issues 1 or 2 days a month and on many days light sinus issues (nasa drip and seezing etc.) only the first hours of the day. But after the dry-fast all sinus issues were gone (it only flared up in presence of animals due to allergy). We had never had any issues with hay fever up to then. However, after the dry-fast, when the fatigue came on, we both started to have very intense allergic reactions in the month May (hay fever). 4,5 weeks every day we have very intense sinus allergies. Could this mean a shift in the immune system? Does this give another clue in our fatigue situation?
>
>
> **Our vision on how to heal (please let us know if you agree):**
> We have had bad genetics from our father (he has the the same sinus allergies and always complains about his gut). His brother (our uncle has the same issues). Therefore we already has a somewhat weakened immune system from very young age. Our Tonsils were removed at age 7 due to inflammation. By eating a standard western our gut became gradually worse, especially since were always hungry due to a double mutation on leptin (we did not gain excess weight and remained skinny though). This increased inflammation, which tasked the adrenal glands.
>
> We probably were exposed to lyme disease and co-infections (we remembered having a number of tick bites in our younger years but no red circle). Duet o our immune system being weak, the lyme started to become chronic in stealth mode. We did notice the last years before the dry-fast, that we needed more recovery time after our workouts. But we did not experience fatigue. We do believe we were living in a state of elevated cortisol.
>
> We ate high amounts of carbs (up to 400 grams per day) in many meals per day (6 times per day). This in combination with big cheat meals in the weekends and a double mutation on Leptin, made our metabolic rate very high. It also made us very bad fat burners.
>
> A week prior to the dry-fast, we started eating only fruit. This made us lose a lot of weight as fruit is very low in calories. We were constantly hungry already. When the fast started, our glycogen was depleted quickly. Due to a high metabolic rate, double mutation on leptin and being bad fat burners, the fast was very stressfull for our body and it started to lean very heavily on already tasked adrenal glands.
>
> At the same time, we also abruptly stoppend taking our corticosteroid medication (Beclometason Nevel and Seretide Diskus). All of this was too much for our adrenal glands and they crashed from an elevated state to a chronic low depletion.
>
>
>
> Right now we believe the adrenal glands cant recupurate due to the lyme and co-infections that constantly task the system. We also wonder if it is possible to become LS because of the inflammation from the infections. Leaky gut cant also heal while Lyme, LR and low adrenals are maintained.
>
> From our point of view, our energy will not come back until the adrenals are healed, which will not happen until the lyme infections are gone.
>
> Step 1: Heal lyme. Would staying in the yucatan for 3 months be enough to do this?
>
> Step 2:When lyme is gone, gut will start healing and then adrenals (and other hormones) will start healing.
>
> Our practioner had put us on 10 weeks Leptin protocol (while we were still in the Netherlands) and afterwards (now 3 weeks ago) adviced us to start 0 carb carnivore. We have tried eating 0 carb for 2 weeks and we started to feel worse. We believe our Leptin could not be posibly healed while Lyme is still there. Which is why 0 carbs still does not work for us.
>
>
> **Any Advice?**
> Perhaps we have totally missed something or perhaps our vision is completely off. We are open to anything that you guys might see as our reason for the chronic fatigue. Thanks.

Hi Thijs, I'm also from the Netherlands. Can you tell me which practioner in the Netherlands is following Dr. Kruse's protocol? I might need some help. Thanks

> [BruceKing said:](/goto/post?id=342739)
>
> rtd, I looked at the NuCalm on their website and it looks like it is simply a binaural beat that supposedly entrains your brain into different brainwave states (research shows no difference between binaural beats and white noise - [https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/22496862/](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/22496862/) ) and an eye mask to put your eyes in complete darkness (Of course, your skin is still exposed to light or you wouldn't need a mask). We all know what the body wants to do in darkness (i.e., sleep) Also, peer reviewed research shows that listening to binaural beats impairs cognitive function - [https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10329717/](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10329717/)
> And, the Bio-Discs seem like complete bullshit. Nothing wrong with a placebo effect, but it is probably cheaper to go outside and listen to nature.

I do not think it is a simple as you state. There is a difference between non-linear oscillating binaural beats and linear. One of the NuCalm research data samples Heart Rate Variability (HRV), the time in between heart beats. Converting the time domain measurements to frequency domain (using a FFT), the ratio of LF/HF expresses parasympathetic dominance vs sympathetic. If you follow anything quantum on this site and postings, energy and frequencies are part of living organisms. I disagree with your provided point of view.

### Thijs Oosting — 2024-11-25T16:00:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-journal-chronic-fatigue-me-and-my-twinbrother.28522/post-342888>

> [Sandra2nd1 said:](/goto/post?id=342767)
>
> Hi Thijs, I'm also from the Netherlands. Can you tell me which practioner in the Netherlands is following Dr. Kruse's protocol? I might need some help. Thanks

Hey Sandra. Look for 'Your Health Concept.' They are active on instagram and have some videos on youtube. I followed their 1 year course and learned the basics of light, water and magnetism and how light sculpts life. The practitionor Thomas van Doorn is really knowledgable, but unfortunately he could not really help me with my chronic fatigue so he advised to book a consult with Jack.. which I did!

I think that thomas is very good in treating things like:
\- skin conditions (like eczema, psoriaris)
\- thyroid issues
\- weight loss
\- gut issues (like IBS, Crohns)
\- and probably a lot more

I dont think Thomas has a lot of experience in treating people who suffer from:
\- Lyme disease
\- Mast Cell Activation syndrome
\- Adrenal Fatigue
\- CFS/ME

I think for these things you should book a consult with Uncle Jack.

>
> You appear to be picking up an electric field on your ethernet network, it needs to be grounded. Is your phone plugged in charging or on battery? NEVER use a phone or laptop/tablet while charging UNLESS the charger is grounded.

how to ground an ethernet cable? when I use my phone, it is cellular off wifi off bluetooth off airplane mode on and I use a stylus instead of touching the screen.

### Anthony Smith — 2024-12-30T20:42:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-journal-chronic-fatigue-me-and-my-twinbrother.28522/post-344493>

If you have Cat6A, Cat7 or Cat 8, all of which are shielded and have metal as part of the plug, you attach a ground cord to the plug. Cat5 has all plastic plugs and you have to ground the ethernet bus. We have a Ethernet to ground plug/cord or just use an alligator clip to ground. If you have an Apple use a grounded power cord and that can sometimes ground the whole system.

### [Grounding cords (inside/12 ft)](https://liveemfsafe.com/store/ols/products/xn-grounding-cord-12-t42j)

12 ft Grounding cords with ground only safe plug With Alligator clip - for bed frames, metal desks, grounding/earthing pads etc. With Ethernet plug to ground Ethernet network With USB to ground computer and/or laptop If you do not have a circuit tester YOU WILL NEED ONE to ground safely.

 liveemfsafe.com

### Thijs Oosting — 2025-04-13T18:54:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-journal-chronic-fatigue-me-and-my-twinbrother.28522/post-350617>

> [Sunny Day said:](/goto/post?id=349398)
>
> [@Thijs Oosting](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/29722/)
> how are you doing now? Did being in Costa Rica help you?

No unfortunately being in Costa Rica did nothing for me and my brother's fatigue. We completed 6 months but are now back in the Netherlands. We are still outside a lot and live a circadian lifestyle, but we do not believe the melanin renovation protocol will heal our chronic fatigue.

We have decided to start on the primal diet by Aajonus Vonderplanitz. among many things he recommends gaining a lot of weight/fat by eating raw animal fats, mainly raw butter and cream. We are going to gain around 15 kilo's of fat. Lets see where it takes us.

Sorry to hear that. I've heard similar from others with ME/CFS. And experienced similar myself to a certain degree.

The dry fast was too much load on an already broken system resulting in a bio electric blow out. This is not just a battery problem the wiring is frayed.. you will need to jump start the system and bypass damage to get things moving again ro restore the 1 pico amp regenerative current.. ### Thijs Oosting — 2025-04-24T12:37:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-journal-chronic-fatigue-me-and-my-twinbrother.28522/post-351193>

I think I finally understand what is going on (to some degree). I was focussing on adrenal fatigue, gut issues and Lyme and co-infections. These things are all true probably and are absolutely the result of a low redox and epigenetic post generational changes of POMC. However, what I failed to understand is that my chronic fatigue, that suddenly started, very abruptly 9 years ago during a 5 day dry-fast, is due to a ‘freeze’ state of my nervous system/brain. According to Dr. Robert Naviaux, ME/CFS is an evolutionary conserved, genetically regulated, hypometabolic state similar to Dauer (hibernation/freeze) that permits survival and persistence under conditions of (perceived) environmental stress but at the cost of severely curtailed function quality of life. Also see Polyvagal theory for a more deeper explanation.

That is what people call Chronic Fatigue Syndrome. This happens when the various stressors in the body, that each undoubtedly are caused by low redox, are perceived as too much by the brain. The brain sees all these different stressors (infections, gut disbalance, HPA Axis dysfunction, fasting, perfectionism, etc) as traumatic to the overall system. When things get too much, the brain/nervous system sort of collapse and get stuck in a form of freeze, whereby the brain/nervous system never allows the body to return to the parasympathetic mode. This collapse is usually preceded by a so called trigger event, i.e. the last drop in the bucket that caused the bucket to spill. In our case it was the 5 day dry-fast but in most CFS cases it is a virus or a flu etc. Long Covid is in fact CFS that was triggered by Covid. When this secondary system goes off-rail (CFS) it causes debilitating fatigue that is not to be confused with other forms of fatigue that are caused by weak mitochondria, infections such as Lyme, gut dysbiosis etc.

Looking at my fatigue from this point, I started to understand that my fatigue is due to a problem in the brainstem, not due to weak mitochondria. This became even more obvious when I started to compare myself to my grandmother.

My grandma is 85 years old and is covered in eczema. She has had surgery for cancer multiple times. She also has type 1 diabetes and various other diseases. She frequently lands in the hospital and when she is able to walk she walks extremely slowly behind a walker. It is very clear to see that her health is absolutely far worse than I am at 36 years old. There is absolutely no way that my grandma’s mitochondria are better than mine. Yet she has more energy than I have. She eagerly waits for visits of family members in between her daily naps whereas I actively avoid visits because they are simply too exhausting. When she is awake, I can see in the way that she moves her weak muscles that the energy behind the movements is greater than mine. I can see that when she loses her balance and almost falls, that the way that she responds, even though her muscles are weak, that she has the energy to respond and rebalance herself. And I can see in her facial reactions that responding like that does not feel extremely fatiguing like it does with me.

This fatigue that I experience is completely different. I absolutely feel that this weakness is not due to my mitochondria being worse than my grandma. I feel very much that my fatigue is due to my nervous system being stuck in survival, causing my body to never, in all those years, reach sufficient states of parasympathetic rest mode. As such, my body is overwhelmed all the time and never replenishes energy and is stuck in a hypometabolic (survival) state.

Another reason why I believe CFS is not due to very weak mitochondria is because of the recovery story of a guy ( which I saw on the CFS health podcast). He was a professional runner competing on a high level. He eventually, very abruptly, was struck with CFS and he went from competing on a high level to being bedbound overnight, not being able to do simple things such as washing his hair because it was too exhausting. Through a program called CFS Health, that works with baseline training (a form of extremely slow pacing), he eventually recovered. According to CFS Health, doing too much on a day, such as washing your hair, can seem too traumatic too a brain/nervous system that is in freeze and can, in weak cases of CFS, result in extreme energy crashes that last weeks. Their approach is therefore to find out what is a appropriate each day, and month in month out very slowly increase activities… slowly re-introducing a brain/nervous system that is in freeze mode to every day activities. This process took a year. After that year, he became 3rd in the world in a running competition. It seems very unlikely to me that a high performer like him crashed his mitochondria and within a year recovered his mitochondria to world champion levels while not focussing on redox at all. He probably did most of his recovery in blue light environment with wifi.

Looking at these stories and others and seeing the differences between me and my grandma, I really believe that focussing on redox is not the first step in recovery for CFS. The main focus for CFS should be to get the brain/nervous system in a state of parasympathetic rest. After that is achieved, I think people should absolutely focus on redox/melanin renovation to truly get the body healthier to make sure the brain/nervous system never collapses, even while under serious stress. Because of course someone with a low redox has per definition more stress on the nervous system and as such the brain/nervous system will be more prone to collapsing.

As such, I see why me going to Costa Rica and working on melanin renovation did not result in more energy at all. I constantly ate sea-food, grounded hours per day and slept grounded during the night, followed circadian rhythm, sun bathed hours per day and otherwise remained outside in the shade. All internet was cabled and my RF levels in the house were between 1 and 10 Uwm2. I also measured AC power, it was low. I also had blue blocking glasses and Iris software for my laptop.

All the time, however, everything I did felt overwhelming. We dragged our bodies off the couch. I absolutely had to force myself to ride on the motorcycle with my brother on the back. Walking to the bike, accelerating and braking while driving already felt too exhausting for us, but we just forced ourselves to go the beach. At the beach we dropped our bodies on the ground and laid there for hours in the sand. Not speaking to each other and trying to regain energy by lying and not moving for hours. Driving back was always exhausting and once back at the house we lay back again for most hours of the day, only moving upright for eating/drinking. This is what our 6 months looked like. An absolute hell but we pushed through because we thought melanin renovation would recharge our mitochondria and give us our energy back.

So yes I agree with Dr Kruse that people with Lyme disease, mold and other causes of fatigue, should work on their redox. However, advising someone with CFS (which could as one of the stressors to the nervous system include things like Lyme disease, mold and adrenal fatigue) to do a serious melanin renovation protocol is way too overwhelming for the CFS patient and does not work directly to create more energy because it does not focus on freeing the brain/nervous system from its freeze state.

Even though programs such as CFS health are pointing in the right direction and do heal people, I believe that they are not efficient at all. It often takes 2-3 years or longer to recover, which usually only works if people have help from people making their meals, doing groceries etc. Doing these things without help is often too much for people with CFS and do not allow them to work on their baseline and progress.

There are other doctors who look at the problem from anther angle. Dr Teitelbaum compares CFS to a braking system in the house. CFS is like a Fuze that blows when too many electricity appliances are used. According to him, alleviating the body from the biggest stressors (not all stressors) that put stress on the body will allow the brain to feel safe again and escape freeze. He uses a SHINE protocol which stand for Sleep, Hormones and hypotension, Infections, Nutrition and exercise (only after 3 months when all other stressors are handled with) to treat CFS. He says that when enough stressors, simultaneously, are dealt with, the braking system in body usually turns on again and the body does not respond to exercise with PEM anymore. As such exercise can be used to rapidly build back to normal energy levels again. He frequently sees mold, lyme disease and other infections as important stressors to look at. Looking at it from this point, it makes sense to work on my systemic mold, lyme disease, H pylori infection and gut dysbiosis. Once all are dealt with, I may be able to alleviate enough stress on the system and hopefully allow my brain to step out of freeze again. Looking at it this way, working on my redox should also help. However, recent lab work by Evan Brand show that I am still dealing with several things. **OAT** **test**: low serotonin, depleted glutathione, Major mold colonization, high oxalates. **GI Map**: H pylori, major dysbiosis, low elastase, extremely high SIGA. As such, my time in Costa Rica has not been able to deal with these issues. Also, when I squint my eyes and look at a blue sky, I can see clear floaters (protein particles) that indicate protein damage due to lyme and co0-infections. All these things show that melanin renovation is not enough. At least not in the timeframe I have been giving it.

There is also another way to look at CFS. Dr. Randall Gates from Gates Brain Health agrees that CFS is a brain that is in freeze, but he brings nuance to the table. He describes it as an underactive Hippocampus and overactive amygdala and uses a supplemental protocol to try to build back the hippocampus which will in turn downregulate the over aroused amygdala, thereby working directly on the brain to turn down the freeze state of the brain and return to normal functioning, allowing the body to achieve normal parasympathetic rest mode again when activities allow it. Studies show people with CFS have low pregnenolone, low serotonin and glutathione in the brain. My lab tests showed that as well. Dr gates uses pregnenolone, active B12 and B9, Saint John’s Wort (a herb that releases serotonin), 5-HTP and NAC (for glutathione) to rebuild the hippocampus and turn down the over aroused amygdala.

I worked with him and this protocol for 8 weeks before I went to Costa Rica but felt no different. At the time I was using a form of pregnenolone from Europe because the brand that he normally used was unavailable for purchase from The Netherlands. As such, he wanted to ship the form of pregnenolone he usually used for his patients (which is a slow release form of pregnenolone by the brand Designs for Health) to me. However, at the time I just had a consult with Dr Jack Kruse and decided to ask the remainder of my money back from Dr Gates and use it to buy a plane ticket to Costa Rica to start with my melanin renovation.

At the moment I wonder if the way that Dr Gates was looking at the problem might have been (almost) effective. Him being a vegan and using way too much supplements, I do not like his general take on health. However I do like the fact that he looks directly at the brain for CFS.

This made me think about another woman (Jennifer Brea) with CFS. Uncle Jack actually wrote an article about her: [https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/myalgic-encephalomyelitis-me-short-jack-kruse/](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/myalgic-encephalomyelitis-me-short-jack-kruse/)

I looked up Jennifer Brea and I learned that next to CFS, she also had Craniocervical instability (CCI) and tethered cord syndrome (TCS). It turns out not too long ago, she was approached by a neurosurgeon who saw her CFS documentary. He told her that he could not help her with her CFS but that she needed surgery for her CCI and TCS. However, after 6 spinal surgeries, her PEM (post exertional malaise) was gone and she was able to rapidly recover from ME/CFS after the surgeries. This is what she wrote herself: “It is now clear that all of my CFS/ME symptoms had a mechanical mechanism: brainstem compression (likely with altered cerebrospinal fluid and cranial blood flow) due to cranial settling and craniocervical instability (CCI), in combination with tethered cord syndrome. Given my remarkable improvements, the centrality of those structural mechanisms is, in my case, undeniable. What remains elusive is the root cause. I know that CCI caused my PEM and other ME symptoms.”

I question what had happened with Jennifer Brea? Were the CCI and TCS simply stressors on the body that caused a fuze to be blown like Teitelbaum describes which put the brain in freeze? Or was it like Brea said: CCI and TCS being a mechanical stressor on the brainstem, which caused the brain to be in freeze mode? And do we also need to take into account the fact that post op surgeries often include progesterone injections.. which could contribute to hippocampus activation resulting in a rebalancing of the entire brain?

Turning back to Dr Gates, I remember him talking about the fact that in his early years after graduation, he was fortunate to work under a doctor for 10 years who came up with the protocol for CFS. He told me he initially tried curing around 100 chronic Lyme disease patients with very expensive antibiotic treatments but that it was not successful. Eventually he came up with the supplemental protocol that aimed at rebooting the hippocampus and downregulating the amygdala which according to Gates was successful for the recovery of 90% of the patients within a relatively short period of time. Although at the time the protocol was not successful for me (perhaps due to not using the correct form of pregnenolone), Gates Dr told me that he had other supplemental options in mind to tweak the protocol. Unfortunately I do not know more about the protocol and I also do not know the name of the Doctor he learned it from. It costs around 4000 dollar to get 10 weeks of coaching from Dr Gates, which is too expensive for me to try again and learn more.

It costs around 4000 dollar to get 10 weeks of coaching from Dr Gates, which is too expensive for me to try again and learn more.

Curious to know,
\- Did you ever trial a zero carb meat-only diet for a period of time? (As opposed to carnivore with eggs and dairy carbs etc)
\- How many hours of darkness you typically get? And have you ever done a trial of hours darkness > hours sunlight?

### Thijs Oosting — 2025-04-24T19:53:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-journal-chronic-fatigue-me-and-my-twinbrother.28522/post-351203>

> [Chlo\_e said:](/goto/post?id=351201)
>
> Curious to know,
> \- Did you ever trial a zero carb meat-only diet for a period of time? (As opposed to carnivore with eggs and dairy carbs etc)
> \- How many hours of darkness you typically get? And have you ever done a trial of hours darkness > hours sunlight?

We have done zero carb meat only diet for 2 years or so. Just meat, fat from the meat and salt. No eggs or dairy. We usually go to bed 2 hours after sunset and wake up with sunrise. What do you mean with a trial of hours darkness > hours sunlight?

We also did methelene blue for some weeks but it only made us a little more fatigued.

### Chlo_e — 2025-04-24T20:24:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-journal-chronic-fatigue-me-and-my-twinbrother.28522/post-351207>

> [Thijs Oosting said:](/goto/post?id=351203)
>
> We have done zero carb meat only diet for 2 years or so. Just meat, fat from the meat and salt. No eggs or dairy. We usually go to bed 2 hours after sunset and wake up with sunrise. What to you mean with a trial of hours darkness > hours sunlight?
>
> We also did methelene blue for some weeks but it only made us a little more fatigued.

Interesting to hear! What protocol did you use for the MB?

To clarify what I mean by 'trial of hours darkness > hours sunlight'- have you ever considered experimenting with the ratio of sunlight to darkness in favour of more hours spent either in darkness or at least with the eyes covered? I have been, and continue to, research melatonin which rises when light is no longer entering the eyes and real-world examples where darkness (or eyes covered) is helping people's health. The pineal melatonin signals repair work throughout the entire body incl. nervous system. However, most people cumulatively over the course of a lifetime spend more time exposed to light (with artificial light compounding the problem). Pineal melatonin released in the absence of light acts differently to mitochondrial melatonin in the presence of IR. There is a reason that most of the body's repair work is done during sleep, when the eyes are closed and the brain is less active. The body knows what to repair and in what order, when it gets the opportunity to do so. It may be something for you to seriously look into and/or experiment with.

### Sunny Day — 2025-04-25T00:25:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-journal-chronic-fatigue-me-and-my-twinbrother.28522/post-351219>

> [Thijs Oosting said:](/goto/post?id=351193)
>
> It is very clear to see that her health is absolutely far worse than I am at 36 years old. There is absolutely no way that my grandma’s mitochondria are better than mine. Yet she has more energy than I have.

Yes I know people aged 75-90 who have way more energy than me. There's no comparison between their level of "tired" and my severely debilitated state from ME/CFS.

but your grandma's mitochondria might still be working better than yours, or in a different way to account for her ability to produce energy like that of a non-ME person. Something needs to explain the drastic difference in energy production. As you know it's not just that we're tired. It's that our cells don't make energy and our bodies cannot recover like normal people. Even when that normal person is 85 years old, they recover better than a young person with severe ME.

A study found that at least some people with ME have fragmented mitochondria. Which is pretty disturbing. Clearly our cells aren't making energy like normal people's are.

The CCI surgery does not have a great track record. There have been a few people who got worse after the surgery, so it's not a safe bet at all. I'm not sure Jen Brea is really totally recovered these days. That update on Medium is a few years old now. Sometimes people get better then get worse. She's no longer in the public eye so we don't know.

### Sunny Day — 2025-04-25T01:42:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-journal-chronic-fatigue-me-and-my-twinbrother.28522/post-351221>

Anyway, thanks for sharing your story. It sounds like you were doing everything you could in costa rica to get better, and it didn't work. This illness is truly brutal. I crash from being out in the sun even early in the day in Texas. I can't imagine the horrible crash and lowering of baseline if I tried to spend all day in tropical sun. I think the fact that some people are severely debilitated needs to be factored into these recommendations. And having lived in various Latin American countries in the past, I know it's more of a walking culture with few to no accomodations for disabled people.

### Mat.I — 2025-04-25T11:26:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-journal-chronic-fatigue-me-and-my-twinbrother.28522/post-351241>

> [Thijs Oosting said:](/goto/post?id=351203)
>
> We also did methelene blue for some weeks but it only made us a little more fatigued.

What dose in mg/kg? Did you vet all the lab tests for purity, heavy metals and formaldehyde etc. Also, you need to do it very carefully at low doses and monitor symptoms and pulse it as required to manage any stalling / ROS build up. I have found creatine is a good buffer that also helps.. MB is the jump start and Creatine helps stabilise and buffer the surges and dips..

I don't think I have heard Jack mention to pair MB with creatine (so not sure about his thoughts on this), however, I stumbled upon this when helping a friend out with a very fragile system and it seemed to work quite well and still is...My initial thought was COQ10, however creatine made more sense for my friends case..

Also today I came across this rumor that the covid vaccines also would calcify the gland.

Inducing more fear, stress and disease rather than healing.

But how about other toxins and the pineal gland?
I just came in from the garden coz I smell pesticides in the air. : (((

I recall Edinger linking the pineal to the gut also. He speaks in biophysical terms.
Very fascinating in any case. Should re-listen. Here's one of his interviews in German:

### Thijs Oosting — 2025-04-26T12:06:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-journal-chronic-fatigue-me-and-my-twinbrother.28522/post-351314>

I made a mistake by saying that my mitochondria aren not worse than my grandma. Yes the mitochondria do not work well in people with CFS. But I think I was right by saying not to focus on the mitochondria for people with CFS because it is the brain that commands the cells to produce less energy by activating a cell danger response due to too much overall stressors on the body.

It is the brain that is in a state of 'freeze,' due to which the brain sends a signal to all cells, causing the cells to reduce it oxidative phosphorylation by 74%.. Dr Robert Navioux calls it a human Dauer syndrome. This is what it says on his CFS laboratory website:

_Research in our laboratory supports the hypothesis that, “ME/CFS is a human dauer syndrome that is maintained by persistent activation of the cell danger response (CDR) through purinergic signaling.” Dauer syndromes are common in Nature as a hypometabolic response to life-threatening environmental factors like infection, trauma, famine, hypoxia, and seasonal change. The biology of dauer is mutlifaceted and ancient and overlaps with hibernation, torpor, estivation, and diapause. Post-chemotherapy and post-COVID (long-hauler) fatigue syndromes, and post-treatment Lyme disease syndrome (PTLDS) tap into the same biology._

The fact that both lyme disease and famine (severe food restriction i.e. fasting) can cause a body to get in a state of dauer syndrome, is interesting in our case. We have allways suspected that we already had lyme disease before the fast triggered our CFS.

The website further describes the changes in mitochondria of people with CFS as follows:

### [Chronic Fatigue Syndrome research – Naviaux Lab](https://naviauxlab.ucsd.edu/science-item/chronic-fatigue-syndrome-research/)

 naviauxlab.ucsd.edu

_Mitochondria are located at the hub of the wheel of metabolism and help coordinate and maintain the metabolic changes found in ME/CFS and dauer. All stressed cells release ATP through channels in the cell membrane. Once outside the cell, ATP binds to receptors called “purinergic receptors” to change gene expression, trigger inflammation, initiate the healing cycle, and decrease mitochondrial oxygen consumption by decreasing oxidative phosphorylation. Direct injection of ATP in mice has been shown to decrease mitochondrial oxygen consumption and energy production by 74% and triggers changes in over 30 different metabolic pathways._

Therefore, it is the cumulative stress (which is different for every CFS patients as it can range from multiple infections, stored emotions, childhood trauma, SNPs, toxins, chemical pressure on the brain itself due to tumors or spine problems, gut dysbiosis, hormonal problems etc.) that in totality are perceived as too overwhelming trauma to a mind/body system. As such the brain, gets stuck in a state of 'freeze' as both fighting the danger nor running away seem an option. This is called a dauer response or CFS.. and the result is a cell danger response in all cells, causing ATP to be send out of all cells and block purinergic receptors in order to cause, among many things, a huge decrease in energy production, in order for the body to play 'death' as a means to survive the ongoing trauma.

As such, working on the health of the mitochondria while the brain is simultaniously trying to decrease the production, does not seem like a good strategy. As far as I understand, the best strategy would be to work directly on the brain/nervous system to try and send signals of safety to the brain. Working on the nervous system is crucial since the nervous system is dysregulated in dauer states.

At the same time, one should work on the stressors that are the biggest players to the brain's perceived overwhelm. I repeatedly see people mentioning unproccesed emotions as a key stressor on the nervous system. However, I think in most CFS cases, a chronic infection is allways at play as well. Lyme, mold, EBV and or mycoplasma seem to be involved in most CFS cases. The reason why I think working on redox for infection such as lyme and mold is not a correct strategy is because a huge part of healing when working on redox is the release of melatonin at night, which I recon does not work due to cortisol spikes when the brain is in a state of unsafety. As such, it might be better to work on infections such as lyme and mold not by focussing on redox but rather by doing herbal protocols, which ironically would in non CFS patient be less effective than a good melanin renovation protocol.

My current strategy is therefore the following:

\- Doing herbal protocol for gut dysbiosis, H pylori infection, Lyme, mold (under guidance of Evan Brand), to adress the biggest stressors on my nervous system.
\- Doing nervous system work (mostly body/brain activities such as breathing exersices or somatic movement but also some brain/body approaches like brain retraining) in order to send signals of safety to my brain.
\- Working on processing stored emotions
\- I am looking into NESA therapy, which seems to have good results for people with CFS.

### [NESA Therapy for Chronic Fatigue & Long Covid](https://nesaclinics.co.uk/chronic-fatigue-long-covid/)

For individuals with Chronic Fatigue Syndrome/Myalgic Encephalomyelitis (CFS/ME) or Long Covid, standing still can trigger symptoms like dizziness, altered vision, nausea, and fatigue.

 nesaclinics.co.uk

Do your own research, however (my basic understanding) -

In chronic CDR, the nervous system can intend to signal safety, but the body’s redox collapse, membrane voltage loss, and biophoton silence physically prevent coherent healing signals from propagating.

Charge precedes consciousness — without restoring mitochondrial electron flow, membrane potential, redox balance, and structured water (sunlight, magnetism), nervous system retraining and infection clearing alone may be ineffective.

In chronic CDR, the brain might "want" to shift out of freeze, but without a functional redox matrix and coherent water structure, the effort cannot fully succeed.

This is where methylene blue and creatine may become essential tools e.g.

Methylene Blue acts as an alternative electron carrier, rescuing electron flow even when Complex I and III are partially disabled. Importantly, it stabilizes electron tunneling rates, supports mitochondrial membrane potential, and partially restores biophoton emission by reactivating cytochrome pathways.

Creatine stabilizes the local ATP:ADP ratio, buffering rapid energy transitions at the membrane, maintaining membrane voltage, and helping to reestablish organized exclusion zone (EZ) water — the foundation for charge storage and regenerative current flow.

If the bioelectric grid is repaired first; then the brain’s safety code be read.

Just my two cents to add to this conversation — hope it helps.

I repeatedly see people mentioning unproccesed emotions as a key stressor on the nervous system. However, I think in most CFS cases, a chronic infection is allways at play as well. Lyme, mold, EBV and or mycoplasma seem to be involved in most CFS cases. The reason why I think working on redox for infection such as lyme and mold is not a correct strategy is because a huge part of healing when working on redox is the release of melatonin at night, which I recon does not work due to cortisol spikes when the brain is in a state of unsafety. As such, it might be better to work on infections such as lyme and mold not by focussing on redox but rather by doing herbal protocols, which ironically would in non CFS patient be less effective than a good melanin renovation protocol.
>
> My current strategy is therefore the following:
>
> \- Doing herbal protocol for gut dysbiosis, H pylori infection, Lyme, mold (under guidance of Evan Brand), to adress the biggest stressors on my nervous system.
> \- Doing nervous system work (mostly body/brain activities such as breathing exersices or somatic movement but also some brain/body approaches like brain retraining) in order to send signals of safety to my brain.
> \- Working on processing stored emotions
> \- I am looking into NESA therapy, which seems to have good results for people with CFS.
>
> ### [NESA Therapy for Chronic Fatigue & Long Covid](https://nesaclinics.co.uk/chronic-fatigue-long-covid/)
>
> For individuals with Chronic Fatigue Syndrome/Myalgic Encephalomyelitis (CFS/ME) or Long Covid, standing still can trigger symptoms like dizziness, altered vision, nausea, and fatigue.
>
>  nesaclinics.co.uk

I hope some of these strategies help move the needle for you both. I'm sure the forum would be interested to hear if you have any breakthroughs down the line. We really do have to become our own guinea pigs and find what works. Best of luck to you and your twin

### Saichi — 2025-04-28T18:19:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-journal-chronic-fatigue-me-and-my-twinbrother.28522/post-351438>

How much time did you spend on the laptop every day? The DE and electric fields within a two foot radius are nasty on them, I never touch my laptop when it's on even when it's grounded.

### Sunny Day — 2025-05-24T20:55:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-journal-chronic-fatigue-me-and-my-twinbrother.28522/post-353275>

> [Thijs Oosting said:](/goto/post?id=351314)
>
> I made a mistake by saying that my mitochondria aren not worse than my grandma. Yes the mitochondria do not work well in people with CFS. But I think I was right by saying not to focus on the mitochondria for people with CFS because it is the brain that commands the cells to produce less energy by activating a cell danger response due to too much overall stressors on the body.
>
> It is the brain that is in a state of 'freeze,' due to which the brain sends a signal to all cells, causing the cells to reduce it oxidative phosphorylation by 74%.. Dr Robert Navioux calls it a human Dauer syndrome. This is what it says on his CFS laboratory website:
>
> _Research in our laboratory supports the hypothesis that, “ME/CFS is a human dauer syndrome that is maintained by persistent activation of the cell danger response (CDR) through purinergic signaling.” Dauer syndromes are common in Nature as a hypometabolic response to life-threatening environmental factors like infection, trauma, famine, hypoxia, and seasonal change. The biology of dauer is mutlifaceted and ancient and overlaps with hibernation, torpor, estivation, and diapause. Post-chemotherapy and post-COVID (long-hauler) fatigue syndromes, and post-treatment Lyme disease syndrome (PTLDS) tap into the same biology._
>
> The fact that both lyme disease and famine (severe food restriction i.e. fasting) can cause a body to get in a state of dauer syndrome, is interesting in our case. We have allways suspected that we already had lyme disease before the fast triggered our CFS.
>
> The website further describes the changes in mitochondria of people with CFS as follows:
>
>
> ### [Chronic Fatigue Syndrome research – Naviaux Lab](https://naviauxlab.ucsd.edu/science-item/chronic-fatigue-syndrome-research/)
>
>  naviauxlab.ucsd.edu
>
>
> _Mitochondria are located at the hub of the wheel of metabolism and help coordinate and maintain the metabolic changes found in ME/CFS and dauer. All stressed cells release ATP through channels in the cell membrane. Once outside the cell, ATP binds to receptors called “purinergic receptors” to change gene expression, trigger inflammation, initiate the healing cycle, and decrease mitochondrial oxygen consumption by decreasing oxidative phosphorylation. Direct injection of ATP in mice has been shown to decrease mitochondrial oxygen consumption and energy production by 74% and triggers changes in over 30 different metabolic pathways._
>
> Therefore, it is the cumulative stress (which is different for every CFS patients as it can range from multiple infections, stored emotions, childhood trauma, SNPs, toxins, chemical pressure on the brain itself due to tumors or spine problems, gut dysbiosis, hormonal problems etc.) that in totality are perceived as too overwhelming trauma to a mind/body system. As such the brain, gets stuck in a state of 'freeze' as both fighting the danger nor running away seem an option. This is called a dauer response or CFS.. and the result is a cell danger response in all cells, causing ATP to be send out of all cells and block purinergic receptors in order to cause, among many things, a huge decrease in energy production, in order for the body to play 'death' as a means to survive the ongoing trauma.
>
> As such, working on the health of the mitochondria while the brain is simultaniously trying to decrease the production, does not seem like a good strategy. As far as I understand, the best strategy would be to work directly on the brain/nervous system to try and send signals of safety to the brain. Working on the nervous system is crucial since the nervous system is dysregulated in dauer states.
>
> At the same time, one should work on the stressors that are the biggest players to the brain's perceived overwhelm. I repeatedly see people mentioning unproccesed emotions as a key stressor on the nervous system. However, I think in most CFS cases, a chronic infection is allways at play as well. Lyme, mold, EBV and or mycoplasma seem to be involved in most CFS cases. The reason why I think working on redox for infection such as lyme and mold is not a correct strategy is because a huge part of healing when working on redox is the release of melatonin at night, which I recon does not work due to cortisol spikes when the brain is in a state of unsafety. As such, it might be better to work on infections such as lyme and mold not by focussing on redox but rather by doing herbal protocols, which ironically would in non CFS patient be less effective than a good melanin renovation protocol.
>
> My current strategy is therefore the following:
>
> \- Doing herbal protocol for gut dysbiosis, H pylori infection, Lyme, mold (under guidance of Evan Brand), to adress the biggest stressors on my nervous system.
> \- Doing nervous system work (mostly body/brain activities such as breathing exersices or somatic movement but also some brain/body approaches like brain retraining) in order to send signals of safety to my brain.
> \- Working on processing stored emotions
> \- I am looking into NESA therapy, which seems to have good results for people with CFS.
>
> ### [NESA Therapy for Chronic Fatigue & Long Covid](https://nesaclinics.co.uk/chronic-fatigue-long-covid/)
>
> For individuals with Chronic Fatigue Syndrome/Myalgic Encephalomyelitis (CFS/ME) or Long Covid, standing still can trigger symptoms like dizziness, altered vision, nausea, and fatigue.
>
>  nesaclinics.co.uk

Please keep us updated on how you're doing.

# Thread: Dengue in tropics
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dengue-in-tropics.28523/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Kendall Toerner — 2024-01-21T00:09:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dengue-in-tropics.28523/post-330419>

I recently got Dengue in Martinique and it was not a fun time. Was sick for about a week.

This has me afraid to go to the topics again or settle down there, as the more severe autoimmune reaction can happen if you get another one of the 4 strains. Not to mention the first time was bad enough. Mosquitos love and have always loved me.

I spent the prior 10 days to first symptoms with full day skin and eyes sun exposure, grounding constantly, no nnEMF, great sleep, and a deep tan. It then came on quite aggressively. Zero energy and severe aching in my hip bones to the point where I had to take Tylenol (something I usually avoid at all costs) to get any sleep.

I can only describe it as losing a week of my life. I couldn't do one single thing, and couldn't feel any comfort, for an entire week. Although it sucked, I certainly didn't get any of the low platelet count issues that many others get (aside from very red swollen gums).

Anyone have any thoughts on avoiding this major issue with living in the tropics?

### Dr Rohen Kapur — 2024-01-21T23:50:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dengue-in-tropics.28523/post-330441>

Zero energy and severe aching in my hip bones to the point where I had to take Tylenol (something I usually avoid at all costs) to get any sleep.
>
> I can only describe it as losing a week of my life. I couldn't do one single thing, and couldn't feel any comfort, for an entire week. Although it sucked, I certainly didn't get any of the low platelet count issues that many others get (aside from very red swollen gums).
>
> Anyone have any thoughts on avoiding this major issue with living in the tropics?

Mosquito net, plug in mosquito repellent and being in the sea all day. I too get loved by the mosquitos and it's annoying. One could take ivermectin prophylactically too.

### Anthony Smith — 2024-01-22T00:01:04+0200

### Marko Pollo — 2024-01-23T02:27:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dengue-in-tropics.28523/post-330493>

If you are a favorite meal of insects, it means you are bleeding light and attracting them. The same thing happens with plants. I remember when I first got here and Jack said if you have high redox, the bugs won't bother you. I thought he was full of prunes then, but completely agree with him now. I live between a pond and river, and irrigated pastures on 2 sides. Plenty of bugs. Never use spray when most do and am usually bare chested all day. Only a couple of bites in many years.

### JanSz — 2024-01-21T22:40:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/education.28525/post-330438>

People pay massive amounts of money studying at universities.
Afterward not able to find employment that would allow them to pay for their cost of education.

It is better idea to
find out what one wants to do to support his/her living
find your future employer
have him fund the cost of required education for that particular work.

\--------

Google is arranging this type of tasks.

### [Students - Google Careers](https://www.google.com/about/careers/applications/students/)

Find internships, jobs, and resources to prepare you for a role a Google — either today or in the future.

 www.google.com

Google offers a variety of programs for students and recent graduates to help them prepare for technical roles.
You can find more information about these programs on the [Google Careers website](https://www.google.com/about/careers/applications/students/).
[You can also learn about Google’s hiring process on the same website](https://www.google.com/about/careers/applications/how-we-hire/) [1](https://www.google.com/about/careers/applications/how-we-hire/).
Additionally, you can visit the [Build Your Future site](https://buildyourfuture.withgoogle.com/events/) to discover Google-sponsored programs, events, and more.

////////////////////
I went thru this type of protocol, way back in (communist) Poland.

My deal was that I had to work for my employer the same length of time that they were paying for my college and my living expenses.
Eventually they were sending me to places where no one else was willing to go.

As far as I can tell, Google has better less costly deals.

///////////////////

### Joseph LeBlanc — 2024-01-22T00:31:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/education.28525/post-330446>

I'm a centralized teacher doing everything I can to lessen the centralization enforced on my students.

I can only do so much though in my position.

If you have kids in a centralized school though, I would make a habit of asking them every single day what they were taught.

Ask them specifically what they are taught relating to diversity, equity, and inclusion (or DEI).

Also, ask them specifically about what they are taught in terms of social-emotional learning (or SEL).

If you don't like their answers, then time to homeschool

### Jack Kruse — 2024-01-22T15:49:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/education.28525/post-330453>

Nature is the best decentralized classroom on the planet.

Go it this school.

### Joseph LeBlanc — 2024-01-22T18:26:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/education.28525/post-330465>

Agreed. I try to bring as much nature into my classroom as I can. My window stays open all day. My classroom window also faces east, so I get direct rays into my room all the time.

There was recently a new school built in our district. Admin asked who would like to go. I took one good look at the exterior of the new building to find it has heavily tinted windows that do not open.

I respectfully declined the opportunity to teach at this brand new school. I will take having my window that can open, over any fancy new lab equipment and lab layouts they can tempt me with at this downgrade of a workplace with its dumb, energy efficient windows.

# Thread: Is genetic testing worthwhile?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-genetic-testing-worthwhile.28527/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BrunoB — 2024-01-21T15:44:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-genetic-testing-worthwhile.28527/post-330427>

Hello good day, what is the real use of genetic tests beyond taking methyl folate in case you detect a bad mutation of MTHFR? And in addition to know bad metabolism of alcohol, coffee ...(that you can check this yourself). Thank you!

### JanSz — 2024-01-21T17:03:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-genetic-testing-worthwhile.28527/post-330432>

> [BrunoB said:](/goto/post?id=330427)
>
> Hello good day, what is the real use of genetic tests beyond taking methyl folate in case you detect a bad mutation of MTHFR? And in addition to know bad metabolism of alcohol, coffee ...(that you can check this yourself). Thank you!

About MTHFR.
This suggestion many years ago worked (most of the times).
Probably good suggestion for most.
For those (few) that it does not work, do not spend $$, just enjoy your life the best you can in current status.

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

I did 23andme test.

The biggest thing that I learned from that test is that my Dad is my Dad.
Newer had any reasons to doubt about that, but it helps.

.....................................

**Dr. Amy Yasko** is a well-known figure in the field of MTHFR gene mutations.
[She has a PhD in Microbiology/Immunology/Infectious Disease and has served on the scientific advisory board for various organizations](https://dramyyasko.com/)[1](https://dramyyasko.com/).
Dr. [Yasko has worked in biotechnology for 15 years and has co-developed the first low-cost DNA synthesizer](https://dramyyasko.com/)[1](https://dramyyasko.com/).

[She has created **The Yasko Protocol**, a guide to help individuals understand their genetics and test results, enabling them to make decisions that align with their health goals](https://dramyyasko.com/)[1](https://dramyyasko.com/)[2](https://dramyyasko.com/yaskoprotocol/).
[This protocol is based on noninvasive test data and DNA Nutrigenomic SNP data](https://dramyyasko.com/yaskoprotocol/)[2](https://dramyyasko.com/yaskoprotocol/).
[It uses information from personal test results to help individuals choose from a range of supplements tailored to their needs](https://dramyyasko.com/yaskoprotocol/)[2](https://dramyyasko.com/yaskoprotocol/).

Dr. [Yasko’s work focuses on understanding a central pathway in the body:](https://dramyyasko.com/yaskoprotocol/)
[The Methylation Cycle,](https://dramyyasko.com/yaskoprotocol/)
[a pathway directly related to a range of health conditions](https://dramyyasko.com/yaskoprotocol/)[2](https://dramyyasko.com/yaskoprotocol/).
[She believes that knowledge is power **and provides her resources at no cost** so that individuals and their doctors can access the knowledge they need](https://dramyyasko.com/yaskoprotocol/)[2](https://dramyyasko.com/yaskoprotocol/).

# Thread: Has Dr Kruse ever shared his thoughts on the Cell Danger Response (CDR)
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/has-dr-kruse-ever-shared-his-thoughts-on-the-cell-danger-response-cdr.28531/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### clemsnwill — 2024-01-23T00:32:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/has-dr-kruse-ever-shared-his-thoughts-on-the-cell-danger-response-cdr.28531/post-330485>

Has Dr Kruse ever shared his thoughts on the Cell Danger Response (CDR), and if or how the CDR is connected to the mitochondrias' role in the fight or flight response?

i searched patreon but no luck finding anything

CDR for reference [https://pierrekorymedicalmusings.com/cp/135343096](https://pierrekorymedicalmusings.com/cp/135343096)

### Jack Kruse — 2024-01-24T16:10:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/has-dr-kruse-ever-shared-his-thoughts-on-the-cell-danger-response-cdr.28531/post-330527>

Yes he has. He has taken the CDR apart and many pieces have been described in the Quilt document.

The CDR results in a change of metabolism. Metabolism, like food, is not a foundation piece of the equation. Quantum bioloigcal process are. The metabolic mismatch between available resources and functional capacity produces a cascade of changes in cellular electron flow, oxygen consumption, redox, membrane fluidity, lipid dynamics, bioenergetics, carbon and sulfur resource allocation, protein folding and aggregation, vitamin availability, metal homeostasis, indole, pterin, 1-carbon and polyamine metabolism, and polymer formation.

All this has been written about in many blog series.

The first wave of signals that occur in a cell consists of the release of metabolic intermediates like ATP and ADP, Krebs cycle intermediates, oxygen, and reactive oxygen species (ROS), and is sustained by purinergic signaling. After the danger has been eliminated or neutralized, a choreographed sequence of anti-inflammatory and regenerative pathways is activated to reverse the CDR and to heal. DHA metabolites like docansanoids and elavanoids are critical there.

**The problem of light stress/nnEMF**

When the CDR persists in chronic fashion due to chronic environmental stress, whole body metabolism and the gut microbiome become chronically disturbed, the collective performance of multiple organ systems is impaired, behavior is changed, and chronic disease results.

Metabolic memory of past stress encounters is stored in the form of altered mitochondrial peptide creation and cellular macromolecule content, resulting in an increase in functional reserve capacity through a process known as mitocellular hormesis.

### La-Shawn Hill — 2024-10-06T23:20:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/has-dr-kruse-ever-shared-his-thoughts-on-the-cell-danger-response-cdr.28531/post-340524>

> [clemsnwill said:](/goto/post?id=330531)
>
> Muchas gracias!

Hello. You asked about Cell Danger Response (CDR) back in January, so you're farther along on the "path to healing" than I am. Can you tell me what's helped you so far? I'm following Dr. Kruse's protocol and have increased my sun exposure while cutting blue light exposure. Well, today my research of electromagnetic hypersensitivity (EHS) led me to Dr. Robert Naviaux and the CDR. I think my body is stuck in CDR, and from my limited research thus far I'm interested in DNRS (dynamic neural retraining system). Have you tried this and, if so, did it help? Do you have any recommendations? I plan to call my health insurance and ask about what procedures or therapies it covers for traumatic brain injury.

# Thread: Why does Icelandic water not cause inflammation in tropics?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-does-icelandic-water-not-cause-inflammation-in-tropics.28534/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 38
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Tarun — 2024-01-23T20:54:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-does-icelandic-water-not-cause-inflammation-in-tropics.28534/post-330513>

My understanding from Jack's posts is that we ought to use the local light, water, magnetism and food.

Eating a pineapple in Switzerland causes chaos. Why does drinking water with a northern European barcode not cause chaos when consumed in low lattitudes?

### Joseph LeBlanc — 2024-01-23T22:49:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-does-icelandic-water-not-cause-inflammation-in-tropics.28534/post-330516>

I think it has a lot to do with pineapples being high in carbs, which when metabolized, yield less ATP

**Less ATP** means the **unfolding of collagen proteins** is done to a **lesser degree**. This hinders the flow of electricity and protonicity in your body bc your semiconductors (collagen-water matrices) are less efficient.

So, basically you are consuming something that decreases your body's energy generating abilities, in an environment that is already low energy (low quantum yield in the far north)

And anytime your body loses energy, disease ensues, bc you need ample energy to fight disease.

I think it also has to do with carbs causing reverse electron flow leading to higher amounts of oxidizing ROS, in an environment where you are making less Vitamin D3 (bc less UV) to deal with the higher rate of oxidation.

Drinking water isn't going to cause the things above. It will only lead to you possibly becoming more hydrated, which is a good thing bc you need to be well hydrated for semiconduction throughout the body to work well (**water** is needed to form the **water**\-collagen matrices, which serve as your semiconductors)

### Ryan Carter — 2024-01-24T00:32:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-does-icelandic-water-not-cause-inflammation-in-tropics.28534/post-330519>

You can likely offset the higher deuterium in the tropics with the sun or use an RO filter. I think this is based on a clinician's viewpoint - if you're healthy, you don't need to use Icelandic water - but if you want to reverse X Y Z disease and have more time elapsed, it could be wiser and worth the investment.

In Switzerland, there is no buffer for the deuterium - harder to "deplete."

### budaev — 2024-01-24T04:55:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-does-icelandic-water-not-cause-inflammation-in-tropics.28534/post-330522>

I conducted an experiment myself, where high latitude water and low latitude water were used for seed germination. High latitude water cultivated seeds had more developed and better root systems. The high latitude water comes from the Altay Mountain at 48 degrees north latitude, which belongs to DDW, while the low latitude water comes from 23 degrees north latitude.At the latitude of 40 degrees north where I live, water at higher latitudes performs better.

### budaev — 2024-01-24T05:02:49+0200

### Tarun — 2024-01-24T22:19:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-does-icelandic-water-not-cause-inflammation-in-tropics.28534/post-330547>

Thanks for the replies folks. Here's my updated interpretation based on your feedback. Let me know if anything is incorrect.

\- Light imprints a barcode into our water and food. This barcode is based on the deuterium content.
\- Water and food near the equator have a higher deuterium content, and this decreases as you move to the poles.
\- While equator water and food have a higher deuterium content, the stronger sun can neutralize the harmful effects.
\- Eating a pineapple (fruit so high deuterium + equator so high deuterium) in Switzerland causes inflammation due to the lack of strong sun to neutralize the deuterium.
\- Food and water with low deuterium are nearly always healthier, so that is why Icelandic water doesn't cause inflammation in the tropics. There are some exceptions, infants need high deuterium.

China, Tibet, likely there is more types of animals producing milk.

From what I came across:

What kind milk? cows, goats, sheep.
Cows: A1 type or A2?

Most conventional dairy brands are produced by western cattle breeds (like Holsteins and Frisians), and these cattle produce A1 milk.

A2 dairy is made by goats, buffalo, sheep, camels, and older cattle breeds (like Guernsey and Jersey).

Human breastmilk contains an A2-type form of beta-casein.

> [Tarun said:](/goto/post?id=330547)
>
> **HIGH LATITUDE WATER HAS LOWER ATOMIC MASS WHICH MAKES MITOCHONDRIAL FUNCTION MORE EFFICIENT**

The smaller an object is, the less strictly it is governed by the laws of classical physics and the more it is subject to quantum effects. So realize that deuterium has double the atomic mass and radius of Hydrogen atom. Is this why mitochondria and cells chose H+ over deuterium 3.8 billion years ago to run its hydrogen heat furnace thag drives life?

A tiny hydrogen atom is a quantum object—sometimes behaving like a particle, sometimes more like a wave. Deuterium, twice as heavy as hydrogen, is less subject to quantum effects. The consequence is that D2O is less destabilized than H2O when squeezed between two hydrophobic surfaces and the hydrogen bonds between water molecules get broken. Because of Einstein's E-mc^2 deuterium decreases energy flux when it is in water.

average men went from 140# in 1900 to 200# now

At this time, I found that in USA in 1900 average men (that came there from Europe had a weight of 140# and women 127#.

The current average weight and height of people in the USA are as follows:
Men:
Average height: 69.0 inches (approximately 175.26 cm)
Average weight: 199.8 pounds (approximately 90.63 kg)
Women:
Average height: 63.5 inches (approximately 161.29 cm)

The average weight of men and women in the late 19th century and early 20th century varied. According to a report from 1898, the average weight of a man was approximately 140 pounds and a woman was around 123 pounds.
\----------
In the early 20th century, the average height for men and women varied across different regions. Here are some details:

For Englishmen, the average height rose from 167 cm (or 5 feet 5 inches) during the Roman occupation (200-410 AD) to 173 cm in the 1100s1.
By the end of the early medieval period, heights had increased to 172 cm, edging closer to heights achieved at the start of the 20th century1.

For British men, the average height at age 21 rose from 167.05 cm (5ft 5in) in 1871-75 to 177.37 cm (5ft 10in) in 1971-752.

A study shows that both men and women have added around 11 cm (4.3 in) to their height since 1914, with the average man now 177.5 cm (5ft 9.8in) tall and the average woman boasting a height of 164.4 cm (5ft 4.7in)3.

### JanSz — 2024-01-27T02:22:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-does-icelandic-water-not-cause-inflammation-in-tropics.28534/post-330629>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=330624)
>
> What happened in 1893?
>
> Might that have something to do with people weight?
>
> Might that change be a good finding instead of a bad one?

In 1893, there were several significant events, **but none of them seem to be directly related to people’s weight**. Here are some of the key events:

-   [The Panic of 1893 caused a large crash on the New York Stock Exchange](https://www.onthisday.com/date/1893)[1](https://www.onthisday.com/date/1893).
-   Thomas A. [Edison finished building his first motion picture studio](https://www.onthisday.com/date/1893)[2](https://www.thoughtco.com/timeline-from-1890-to-1900-1774042).
-   [The original Ferris Wheel, designed and built by George Washington Gale Ferris Jr., was opened as the centerpiece of the Midway at the 1893 World’s Columbian Exposition in Chicago, Illinois](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ferris_Wheel_%281893%29)[3](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ferris_Wheel_%281893%29).
-   [New Zealand became the first country to grant all women the right to vote](https://www.onthisday.com/date/1893)[1](https://www.onthisday.com/date/1893).

As for the connection to people’s weight, **it’s possible you’re referring to a specific event or invention in 1893 that had an impact on people’s health or lifestyle, but without more specific information, it’s difficult to provide a precise answer.**
